This trade-off is visualized by the **Precision-Recall (PR) Curve**, where the **Area Under the Curve (AUC-PR)** summarizes model performance across all thresholds. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Taxonomy of Evaluation Metrics This diagram maps the decision flow from model output to the correct evaluation metric, with their financial applications. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Model Output"] --> B{"Prediction Type?"} B -- "Continuous Value" --> C["REGRESSION METRICS"] B -- "Discrete Class Label" --> D["CLASSIFICATION METRICS"] C --> C1["MSE"] C1 --> C2["RMSE"] C --> C3["MAE"] C --> C4["MAPE / R-Squared"] C2 --> F1["Used in Stock Price / Volatility Forecasts"] C3 --> F2["Used in Robust Risk Models with Outliers"] C4 --> F3["Used in Portfolio Return Modelling"] D --> E["Build CONFUSION MATRIX 2x2"] E --> E1["TP"] E --> E2["FP"] E --> E3["FN"] E --> E4["TN"] E1 --> G1["Accuracy"] E2 --> G2["Precision"] E3 --> G3["Recall"] E4 --> G4["Specificity"] G2 --> H1["Harmonic Mean"] G3 --> H1 H1 --> H2["F1-Score"] G1 --> I1["Balanced Datasets Only"] G2 --> I2["Fraud Alerts Quality Control"] G3 --> I3["Default / Risk Coverage"] G4 --> I4["Loan Approval Filtering"] classDef reg fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 classDef cls fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 classDef root fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000 class C,C1,C2,C3,C4,F1,F2,F3 reg class D,E,E1,E2,E3,E4,G1,G2,G3,G4,H1,H2,I1,I2,I3,I4 cls class A,B root ``` ## 4.2 The Confusion Matrix — Annotated Architecture The 2x2 structure with all derived metrics visualized. This is the **canonical KTU diagram** that examiners expect in answers. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Actual_Pos["ACTUAL: FRAUD = POSITIVE"] AP1["Real Fraudsters in World"] end subgraph Actual_Neg["ACTUAL: LEGITIMATE = NEGATIVE"] AN1["Real Genuine Transactions"] end AP1 --> P1["Model Predicts FRAUD"] AP1 --> P2["Model Predicts LEGITIMATE"] AN1 --> P3["Model Predicts FRAUD"] AN1 --> P4["Model Predicts LEGITIMATE"] P1 --> N1["TP = 40
Caught the thief"] P2 --> N2["FN = 10
MISSED FRAUD
Money Lost"] P3 --> N3["FP = 30
False Alarm
Customer Annoyed"] P4 --> N4["TN = 920
Clean Pass"] N1 --> M1["Recall = TP / TP+FN = 40/50 = 0.80"] N2 --> M1 N1 --> M2["Precision = TP / TP+FP = 40/70 = 0.57"] N3 --> M2 N1 --> M3["F1 = 2PR/P+R = 0.67"] N2 --> M3 N3 --> M3 N4 --> M4["Specificity = TN/TN+FP = 920/950 = 0.97"] N3 --> M4 N1 --> M5["Accuracy = TP+TN / Total = 960/1000 = 0.96"] N4 --> M5 N2 --> M5 N3 --> M5 classDef good fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000 classDef bad fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000 classDef metric fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 class N1,N4 good class N2,N3 bad class M1,M2,M3,M4,M5 metric ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flow — Which Metric Should I Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["You have a trained model"] --> Q1{"Is the target a NUMBER
or a CATEGORY?"} Q1 -- "NUMBER e.g. Stock Price" --> R1["Use REGRESSION METRICS"] Q1 -- "CATEGORY e.g. Fraud / Not Fraud" --> C1["Use CLASSIFICATION METRICS"] R1 --> R2{"Are there extreme
outliers in data?"} R2 -- "Yes e.g. crash days" --> R3["MAE is more robust"] R2 -- "No / Few" --> R4["Use RMSE standard"] C1 --> C2{"Are classes
balanced?"} C2 -- "Yes 50:50 approx" --> C3["Accuracy is reliable"] C2 -- "No imbalanced e.g. 1% fraud" --> C4["DO NOT trust Accuracy"] C4 --> C5{"What is costlier?
FP or FN?"} C5 -- "FP e.g. false fraud alert" --> C6["Optimize PRECISION"] C5 -- "FN e.g. missed fraud" --> C7["Optimize RECALL"] C5 -- "Both matter equally" --> C8["Optimize F1-Score"] classDef root fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000 classDef reg fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 classDef cls fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000 class Start,Q1 root class R1,R2,R3,R4 reg class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6,C7,C8 cls ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Define the following terms in the context of financial data science model evaluation:** **(a) RMSE, (b) Confusion Matrix, (c) F1-Score.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per clean definition):** **(a) RMSE (Root Mean Square Error):** It is the square root of the mean of the squared differences between the actual and predicted values of a regression model. $$ \text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2} $$ It is widely used in financial forecasting (e.g., stock price prediction) because it is expressed in the same unit as the target variable (e.g., ₹) and penalizes large forecasting errors more heavily. **(b) Confusion Matrix:** It is a 2×2 tabular representation of a binary classification model's predictions against the true labels, containing the counts of True Positives (TP), False Positives (FP), True Negatives (TN), and False Negatives (FN). It serves as the foundation for computing Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score — critical in credit scoring and fraud detection. **(c) F1-Score:** It is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, providing a single balanced metric especially useful when the dataset is imbalanced (e.g., fraud cases being only 0.1% of transactions). $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} $$ --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **Differentiate between Precision and Recall. Why is Accuracy alone insufficient for evaluating fraud detection models?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Precision** is the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations (TP) to the total predicted positives (TP + FP). It answers: *"Of all transactions flagged as fraud, how many were actually fraud?"* $$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$ **Recall** (Sensitivity) is the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations (TP) to all actual positives (TP + FN). It answers: *"Of all real fraud cases, how many did we catch?"* $$ \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$ **Why Accuracy is insufficient:** Fraud datasets are highly imbalanced (e.g., 99.9% legitimate, 0.1% fraud). A naive model that predicts "Not Fraud" for *every* transaction achieves **99.9% Accuracy** but **0% Recall** — it catches no fraudsters at all. This is the **Accuracy Paradox**. Recall is the appropriate metric because missing a fraud case results in direct monetary loss to the bank. --- ## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Students must answer **either** OR based on the module internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below. --- ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` **A Logistic Regression model was deployed by a bank to predict loan default. On a test set of 1000 applicants, the following results were obtained:** | | Predicted: No Default | Predicted: Default | |---|---|---| | **Actual: No Default** | 850 | 50 | | **Actual: Default** | 30 | 70 | **(a)** Construct the **Confusion Matrix** and compute the **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Specificity** of the model. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Accuracy Paradox** in the context of imbalanced financial datasets. If the bank cares more about catching defaulters than false alarms, which metric should be prioritized, and why? Suggest one strategy to improve model performance on the minority class. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Metric Computation (7 marks)** **Step 1:** Extract values from the matrix. $$ TP = 70, \quad FP = 50, \quad FN = 30, \quad TN = 850 $$ **Step 2:** Verify total. $$ 70 + 50 + 30 + 850 = 1000 \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 3:** Compute **Accuracy**. $$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{70 + 850}{1000} = \frac{920}{1000} = 0.92 \;\; (92\%) $$ **Step 4:** Compute **Precision**. $$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{70}{70 + 50} = \frac{70}{120} \approx 0.5833 \;\; (58.33\%) $$ **Step 5:** Compute **Recall**. $$ \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{70}{70 + 30} = \frac{70}{100} = 0.70 \;\; (70\%) $$ **Step 6:** Compute **F1-Score**. $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \frac{(0.5833)(0.70)}{0.5833 + 0.70} $$ $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.4083}{1.2833} = 2 \cdot 0.3182 \approx 0.6364 \;\; (63.64\%) $$ **Step 7:** Compute **Specificity**. $$ \text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{850}{850 + 50} = \frac{850}{900} \approx 0.9444 \;\; (94.44\%) $$ | Metric | Value | |---|---| | Accuracy | 92.00% | | Precision | 58.33% | | Recall | 70.00% | | F1-Score | 63.64% | | Specificity | 94.44% | --- **Part (b) — Conceptual & Strategic Analysis (7 marks)** **The Accuracy Paradox (3 marks):** In imbalanced financial datasets (e.g., loan default where only ~10% actually default), a trivial "lazy" classifier that predicts "No Default" for *every* applicant would achieve 90% Accuracy, yet be commercially useless. Accuracy treats every cell of the confusion matrix equally, ignoring that **missing a defaulter (FN)** is *quantitatively* and *qualitatively* different from **falsely flagging a good customer (FP)**. Accuracy masks the model's failure on the minority class — the very class of business interest. **Metric Prioritization (2 marks):** Since the bank cares more about catching defaulters, **Recall (Sensitivity)** should be prioritized. A higher Recall reduces **False Negatives**, ensuring fewer defaulters slip through the credit pipeline, which directly protects the bank's capital adequacy ratio (per Basel III norms). **Improvement Strategy (2 marks):** One effective technique is **SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique)** — it generates synthetic examples of the minority "Default" class to balance the training set. Alternative strategies include **class-weight balancing** (penalizing misclassification of the minority class more heavily in the loss function), **threshold tuning** (lowering the default 0.5 threshold to e.g. 0.3), and **ensemble methods** like Balanced Random Forests. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` **(a)** A time-series forecasting model (ARIMA) was used to predict the monthly closing price (in ₹) of an NBFC's stock over 6 months. The data is given below: | Month ($i$) | Actual Price $y_i$ (₹) | Predicted Price $\hat{y}_i$ (₹) | |---|---|---| | 1 | 250 | 245 | | 2 | 260 | 270 | | 3 | 255 | 250 | | 4 | 270 | 265 | | 5 | 280 | 285 | | 6 | 275 | 270 | Compute the **MSE** and **RMSE** of the model. Comment on the practical interpretation of the RMSE in rupees. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain why **RMSE is preferred over MSE** in financial reporting. Under what condition would you recommend **MAE** over RMSE? Justify with a financial example. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — RMSE Computation (7 marks)** **Step 1:** Compute residuals $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$. $$ \begin{aligned} e_1 &= 250 - 245 = +5 \\ e_2 &= 260 - 270 = -10 \\ e_3 &= 255 - 250 = +5 \\ e_4 &= 270 - 265 = +5 \\ e_5 &= 280 - 285 = -5 \\ e_6 &= 275 - 270 = +5 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Square each residual. $$ \begin{aligned} e_1^2 &= 25, \quad e_2^2 = 100, \quad e_3^2 = 25 \\ e_4^2 &= 25, \quad e_5^2 = 25, \quad e_6^2 = 25 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Sum the squared residuals. $$ \sum e_i^2 = 25 + 100 + 25 + 25 + 25 + 25 = 225 $$ **Step 4:** Compute MSE. $$ \text{MSE} = \frac{225}{6} = 37.5 \; (\text{₹}^2) $$ **Step 5:** Compute RMSE. $$ \text{RMSE} = \sqrt{37.5} \approx 6.124 \; \text{₹} $$ **Practical Interpretation (within the 7 marks):** The model's monthly closing price predictions are, on average, off by **₹6.12 per share**. Given the stock trades around ₹265 on average, this is a relative error of $\frac{6.12}{265} \approx 2.3\%$ — generally acceptable. Note that a single error of ₹10 in Month 2 contributed $100/225 \approx 44.4\%$ of the total squared error, illustrating RMSE's **outlier-sensitivity**. --- **Part (b) — RMSE vs MSE vs MAE (7 marks)** **Why RMSE is preferred over MSE (3 marks):** 1. **Unit Interpretability:** MSE is in squared units (₹²), which has no direct physical meaning. RMSE restores the original unit (₹), making it directly comparable with the stock price. 2. **Stakeholder Communication:** A risk manager or portfolio head can intuitively grasp "the model is off by ₹6.12 per share" — not "off by 37.5 rupees squared." 3. **Comparative Magnitude:** When comparing two models, RMSE retains the *scale* of the error, whereas MSE inflates it quadratically, distorting visual intuition in plots. **When MAE is Preferred (2 marks):** MAE is preferred when the dataset contains **extreme outliers** (e.g., black-swan events like the 2008 financial crisis, March 2020 COVID crash, or sudden RBI policy shocks). Because RMSE squares the residuals, a single ₹200 error on a day when the actual price was ₹1 (a 99% crash) would **dominate** the entire score, making the model look terrible even if it performed flawlessly on 99.9% of other days. **Financial Example (2 marks):** Consider **Value-at-Risk (VaR) modeling** for a derivatives portfolio during a crisis period. If the model is tested on a dataset containing the COVID-19 crash, RMSE will be heavily skewed by the extreme residuals of March 2020, giving a misleading view of typical daily performance. **MAE**, which uses absolute values (no squaring), treats all errors **linearly** and provides a more *robust* and *representative* estimate of the model's *typical* error. For daily risk reports, MAE is therefore often used as a sanity check alongside RMSE. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Precision/Recall definitions:** Many students swap FP and FN. Remember: Precision is in the **denominator** of **predicted** positives (column), Recall uses **actual** positives (row). Mnemonic: **"Precision = predicted; Recall = real."** > 2. **Forgetting to declare the Positive Class:** In fraud/default detection, always state *"Positive Class = Fraud"* before computing. Examiners deduct 1 mark for omission. > 3. **Using Accuracy on imbalanced data:** This is the most common conceptual error. If the dataset has < 20% minority class, **never** quote Accuracy as the primary metric — always use F1, Recall, or Precision. > 4. **Unit Confusion in RMSE:** Students often write "RMSE = 37.5" forgetting to take the square root. The unit *must* be in the original (₹), not ₹². > 5. **Reporting Recall as "1 - FN rate":** This is mathematically true but loses 1 mark in KTU. Always compute directly: $TP / (TP + FN)$. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Bullet Summary for Last-Minute Revision:** - ✅ **RMSE** = square root of average squared errors. **Unit = same as target** (₹, %, points). Penalizes outliers heavily. - ✅ **MAE** = average of absolute errors. **Unit = same as target**. **Robust to outliers**. - ✅ **Confusion Matrix** = 2×2 grid: **TP, FP, FN, TN**. Foundation for all classification metrics. - ✅ **Accuracy** = $(TP + TN) / \text{Total}$. **Useful only for balanced classes**. - ✅ **Precision** = $TP / (TP + FP)$. Use when **False Positives are costly** (fraud false alarms). - ✅ **Recall** = $TP / (TP + FN)$. Use when **False Negatives are costly** (missed fraud/defaults). - ✅ **F1-Score** = $2PR / (P + R)$. **Harmonic mean**. Single number for imbalanced classes. - ✅ **Specificity** = $TN / (TN + FP)$. Used in **loan approval** (avoid rejecting good customers). - ✅ **The Accuracy Paradox:** A 99% accurate model can be useless if the 1% it misses is the critical class. - ✅ **Financial Applications Map:** - *Stock price forecasting* → **RMSE** - *Credit default / Fraud* → **Recall + F1** - *Loan approval* → **Specificity** - *VaR with crisis data* → **MAE** (robust) - ✅ **Always declare the Positive Class** in classification problems before computing. - ✅ **Threshold tuning** shifts the Precision-Recall trade-off — choose based on business cost matrix.

topics.title: Implementing a Credit Risk Scoring Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT742 - FINANCIAL DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementing a Credit Risk Scoring Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Credit Risk Scoring Model** is a supervised statistical and/or machine learning system that quantifies the likelihood that a borrower (retail or corporate) will fail to meet contracted debt obligations over a specified horizon, typically expressed as the **Probability of Default (PD)**. The output is a numerical score, calibrated to a target odds range, that segregates the applicant pool into risk buckets enabling automated approval, rejection, pricing, and limit-setting decisions.

The model sits at the heart of the **Expected Loss (EL)** framework governed by the **Basel II/III Accord (IRB Approach)**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Basel II Accord Formulation:**
> $$\text{EL} = \text{PD} \times \text{LGD} \times \text{EAD}$$
> where **PD** = Probability of Default, **LGD** = Loss Given Default, **EAD** = Exposure at Default.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Triage" View

Imagine a hospital emergency ward with 1,000 patients arriving every hour. Doctors cannot spend equal time on every patient, so they build a **triage scoring system**: a quick blood-pressure + symptom + age questionnaire that outputs a number. Patients scoring above 80 are rushed to the ICU; below 30 are sent home with paracetamol. A credit scoring model does **the exact same triage** for loan applicants — but instead of "heart attack risk", it predicts "default risk". The hospital doesn't need to *cure* the patient at triage; it just needs to **rank-order urgency**. Similarly, a credit score need not be a perfect predictor, only a **discriminator** that ranks bad borrowers worse than good borrowers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** A credit score is fundamentally a *ranking device*, not an absolute oracle. The financial industry tolerates some miscalibration in the score as long as the rank-ordering is preserved (this is why the AUC-ROC and KS Statistic are central to evaluation).

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary (Standard KTU Glossary)

| Term | Expansion | Operational Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **PD** | Probability of Default | Probability borrower defaults within 12 months (industry default horizon) |
| **LGD** | Loss Given Default | Fraction of exposure unrecovered post-default (typically 0.45 for unsecured retail) |
| **EAD** | Exposure at Default | Outstanding balance at moment of default |
| **WoE** | Weight of Evidence | Log-odds ratio of goods to bads in a feature bin |
| **IV** | Information Value | Σ(Good% − Bad%) × WoE — measures predictive power |
| **KS** | Kolmogorov–Smirnov Statistic | Maximum vertical distance between cumulative good and bad distributions |
| **Gini** | Gini Coefficient | 2 × AUC − 1, measures discriminatory power |
| **PSI** | Population Stability Index | Detects score distribution drift between development and production samples |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Pipeline of Implementation

A production-grade credit scoring model is built through a deterministic pipeline. Each stage has measurable acceptance criteria before downstream work begins.

### Stage 1 — Problem Definition & Target Window
- Define **"Default"** explicitly (e.g., 90+ days past due — Basel standard).
- Set observation point (T₀) and outcome window (typically **12 months**).
- Determine **population scope**: new accounts only, or behaviour scoring for existing portfolio?

### Stage 2 — Data Acquisition & Sampling
- Pull from Core Banking System, Credit Bureau, Internal Behavioural DB.
- Handle the **class imbalance problem**: default rate is usually **2 % – 8 %** in retail portfolios.

### Stage 3 — Feature Engineering
- **Application features**: age, income, employment length, loan amount, tenure.
- **Bureau features**: number of active trades, delinquency count, oldest account.
- **Behavioural features**: average balance, utilisation trend, recent enquiry velocity.
- **Derived ratios**: Debt-to-Income (DTI), Loan-to-Income (LTI), Credit Utilisation Ratio (CUR).

### Stage 4 — Binning & Weight of Evidence (WoE) Transformation
- Continuous variables are split into monotonic bins (≤ 5 bins for stability).
- Each bin receives a **WoE value**, replacing the raw feature.
- The transformed feature becomes linearly correlated with log-odds of default — the critical assumption of logistic regression scorecards.

### Stage 5 — Model Training
- **Logistic Regression** dominates regulatory environments (interpretability).
- **Gradient Boosting (XGBoost / LightGBM)** dominates in private fintech (accuracy).
- KTU expects both paradigms in the answer script.

### Stage 6 — Validation, Calibration & Deployment
- Out-of-Time (OOT) and Out-of-Sample (OOS) validation.
- **Calibration plot** (predicted PD vs observed default rate by decile).
- **PSI < 0.10** for production sign-off; **PSI > 0.25** triggers model rebuild.

## 2.2 The WoE & IV Derivation Logic

For a feature $X$ binned into $k$ categories, define:

- $G_i$ = number of **good** borrowers in bin $i$
- $B_i$ = number of **bad** borrowers in bin $i$
- $\text{Dist}_{\text{Good},i} = G_i / \sum G$
- $\text{Dist}_{\text{Bad},i} = B_i / \sum B$

The **Weight of Evidence** for bin $i$ is:

$$\text{WoE}_i = \ln\!\left(\frac{\text{Dist}_{\text{Good},i}}{\text{Dist}_{\text{Bad},i}}\right)$$

The **Information Value** of the feature is:

$$\text{IV} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left(\text{Dist}_{\text{Good},i} - \text{Dist}_{\text{Bad},i}\right) \times \text{WoE}_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IV Predictive Power Reference (Industry Standard):**
> - IV < 0.02 → Not predictive (drop feature)
> - 0.02 ≤ IV < 0.1 → Weak predictor
> - 0.1 ≤ IV < 0.3 → Medium predictor
> - 0.3 ≤ IV < 0.5 → Strong predictor
> - IV ≥ 0.5 → Suspiciously high (likely over-predicting, investigate for leakage)

## 2.3 The Logistic Regression Scorecard Equation

After fitting a logistic regression on WoE-transformed features, the model produces coefficients $\beta_0, \beta_1, \dots, \beta_n$. The **Probability of Default** for an applicant is:

$$p = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{WoE}_1 + \beta_2 \text{WoE}_2 + \cdots + \beta_n \text{WoE}_n)}}$$

To convert this probability into a human-readable scorecard, two scaling parameters are used — **Points-to-Double-Odds (PDO)** and **Target Score (S₀)**:

$$\text{Score} = \text{Offset} + \text{Factor} \times \ln(\text{Odds})$$

$$\text{where} \quad \text{Factor} = \frac{\text{PDO}}{\ln(2)}, \quad \text{Offset} = S_0 - \text{Factor} \times \ln(\text{Odds}_0)$$

For each feature, the score contribution of bin $b$ is:

$$\text{Score}_{b} = -\left(\beta_j\right)\left(\text{WoE}_{b}\right) \times \text{Factor} + \frac{\text{Offset}}{n_{\text{features}}}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> All standard formulas required for KTU ESE 2024 Scheme questions on this topic. All pipes use `\vert` to preserve table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expected Loss | $\text{EL} = \text{PD} \times \text{LGD} \times \text{EAD}$ | Basel capital calculation |
| 2 | WoE (bin) | $\text{WoE}_i = \ln\!\left(\frac{\text{Dist}_{\text{Good},i}}{\text{Dist}_{\text{Bad},i}}\right)$ | Feature transformation |
| 3 | Information Value | $\text{IV} = \sum (\text{Dist}_{\text{Good}} - \text{Dist}_{\text{Bad}}) \times \text{WoE}$ | Feature selection |
| 4 | Logistic PD | $p = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \; z = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i$ | Probability estimation |
| 5 | Log-Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum [y\ln p + (1-y)\ln(1-p)]$ | Model optimisation |
| 6 | KS Statistic | $\text{KS} = \max \vert F_{\text{Good}}(x) - F_{\text{Bad}}(x) \vert$ | Discrimination check |
| 7 | Gini | $G = 2 \times \text{AUC} - 1$ | Discrimination power |
| 8 | PSI | $\text{PSI} = \sum (A_i - E_i)\ln(A_i / E_i)$ | Drift monitoring |
| 9 | Accuracy | $\frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{TN} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}}$ | Baseline metric |
| 10 | Precision | $\frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | Cost-of-false-positive |
| 11 | Recall | $\frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | Default capture rate |
| 12 | F1-Score | $2 \times \frac{P \times R}{P + R}$ | Imbalance-friendly |
| 13 | AUC-ROC | $\int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}^{-1}(t))\,dt$ | Threshold-free rank metric |
| 14 | DTI Ratio | $\frac{\text{Total Monthly Debt}}{\text{Monthly Gross Income}}$ | Applicant feature |
| 15 | Score Offset | $\text{Offset} = S_0 - \frac{\text{PDO}}{\ln 2} \times \ln(\text{Odds}_0)$ | Scorecard scaling |
| 16 | Score Factor | $\text{Factor} = \frac{\text{PDO}}{\ln 2}$ | Scorecard scaling |
| 17 | Bin Score | $-\beta_j \times \text{WoE}_b \times \text{Factor} + \frac{\text{Offset}}{n}$ | Per-bin points |
| 18 | Sharpe-Loss | $\text{SL} = \frac{\text{Portfolio Return} - r_f}{\sigma}$ | (Adjacent topic) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Credit scoring systems are the **central nervous system** of every consumer lending institution. The FICO score (USA) and CIBIL TransUnion Score (India) underpin **trillions of dollars** in lending decisions. Beyond banking, the same WoE-IV-logistic pipeline is used in:
- **Insurance underwriting** (claim propensity scoring)
- **Telecom churn prediction**
- **Fraud detection** (re-purposed as anomaly scorecard)
- **Supply-chain finance** (supplier default scoring)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Building a Mini Scorecard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A regional bank has 2,000 past loan records, of which 1,800 are "Good" (non-default) and 200 are "Bad" (default). The feature **"Number of Credit Cards"** is binned into 3 categories. Derive WoE, IV, and assess predictive power.

### Step 1: Tabulate the Raw Counts

| Number of Credit Cards (Bin) | Good ($G_i$) | Bad ($B_i$) | Total |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 – 1 | 1,200 | 40 | 1,240 |
| 2 – 3 | 450 | 80 | 530 |
| ≥ 4 | 150 | 80 | 230 |
| **Total** | **1,800** | **200** | **2,000** |

### Step 2: Compute Distribution Percentages

**Good distribution:**

$$\text{Dist}_{\text{Good},1} = \frac{1200}{1800} = 0.6667, \quad \text{Dist}_{\text{Good},2} = \frac{450}{1800} = 0.2500, \quad \text{Dist}_{\text{Good},3} = \frac{150}{1800} = 0.0833$$

**Bad distribution:**

$$\text{Dist}_{\text{Bad},1} = \frac{40}{200} = 0.2000, \quad \text{Dist}_{\text{Bad},2} = \frac{80}{200} = 0.4000, \quad \text{Dist}_{\text{Bad},3} = \frac{80}{200} = 0.4000$$

### Step 3: Compute WoE for Each Bin

$$\text{WoE}_1 = \ln\!\left(\frac{0.6667}{0.2000}\right) = \ln(3.3335) = 1.2040$$

$$\text{WoE}_2 = \ln\!\left(\frac{0.2500}{0.4000}\right) = \ln(0.6250) = -0.4700$$

$$\text{WoE}_3 = \ln\!\left(\frac{0.0833}{0.4000}\right) = \ln(0.2083) = -1.5686$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Positive WoE in bin 1 means "more goods than bads" — this bin is **safer**. Negative WoE in bins 2 and 3 indicates **higher risk**, with bin 3 (≥ 4 cards) being the worst. The WoE is monotonically decreasing, satisfying the scorecard monotonicity assumption.

### Step 4: Compute Information Value (IV)

$$\text{IV}_1 = (0.6667 - 0.2000) \times 1.2040 = 0.4667 \times 1.2040 = 0.5619$$

$$\text{IV}_2 = (0.2500 - 0.4000) \times (-0.4700) = (-0.1500) \times (-0.4700) = 0.0705$$

$$\text{IV}_3 = (0.0833 - 0.4000) \times (-1.5686) = (-0.3167) \times (-1.5686) = 0.4967$$

$$\text{IV}_{\text{Total}} = 0.5619 + 0.0705 + 0.4967 = 1.1291$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Examiner Observation:** An IV of 1.13 is **suspiciously high** (≥ 0.5 threshold breached). In a KTU answer, you must explicitly state this triggers an **over-prediction / leakage check**. Common causes: target leakage, look-ahead bias, or simply too few bads per bin. Industry practice caps individual feature IV at **0.5** and total feature-engineered IV accordingly.

### Step 5: Logistic PD on a New Applicant

Suppose the fitted model yields $\beta_0 = -2.5$, $\beta_1 = -0.8$ (coefficient for "Number of Credit Cards"). A new applicant has 5 credit cards (bin 3, WoE = −1.5686).

$$z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{WoE}_3 = -2.5 + (-0.8)(-1.5686) = -2.5 + 1.2549 = -1.2451$$

$$p = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(-1.2451)}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{1.2451}} = \frac{1}{1 + 3.4732} = \frac{1}{4.4732} = 0.2236$$

So the applicant's **PD ≈ 22.36 %** — substantially higher than the portfolio average of 10 %, which flags the application for manual review or higher pricing.

### Step 6: Convert to Scorecard Points (PDO = 20, Target Score = 600 at odds 50:1)

$$\text{Factor} = \frac{20}{\ln 2} = \frac{20}{0.6931} = 28.8539$$

$$\text{Offset} = 600 - 28.8539 \times \ln(50) = 600 - 28.8539 \times 3.9120 = 600 - 112.88 = 487.12$$

For the applicant's bin 3 (WoE = −1.5686, with this single feature only, $n = 1$):

$$\text{Score} = -(-0.8) \times (-1.5686) \times 28.8539 + \frac{487.12}{1} = -36.21 + 487.12 = 450.91$$

The applicant scores **~451**, well below the typical cutoff of 580 — application is rejected.

## 3.2 Derivation of the KS Statistic

The KS statistic compares the empirical cumulative distribution functions (ECDFs) of the predicted scores for goods and bads.

$$F_{\text{Good}}(x) = \frac{1}{N_G} \sum_{i: s_i \le x, y_i = 0} 1, \qquad F_{\text{Bad}}(x) = \frac{1}{N_B} \sum_{i: s_i \le x, y_i = 1} 1$$

$$\text{KS} = \max_{x} \vert F_{\text{Good}}(x) - F_{\text{Bad}}(x) \vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Acceptance Threshold:** KS ≥ 0.40 is generally considered an acceptable scorecard for retail portfolios in KTU-style numerical problems.

## 3.3 Confusion Matrix Economics

Beyond statistical metrics, lenders use a **cost-weighted** evaluation:

$$\text{Expected Decision Cost} = C_{\text{FP}} \cdot \text{FP} + C_{\text{FN}} \cdot \text{FN}$$

For a retail lender:
- **False Negative** (predicted good, actual bad) → loan loss = $\approx$ ₹50,000 (1-year LGD × EAD)
- **False Positive** (predicted bad, actual good) → lost interest = $\approx$ ₹8,000

The optimal threshold is therefore **NOT 0.5** but the value that minimises the expected cost — a frequent KTU sub-question.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Credit Scoring Pipeline (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Applicant Data] --> B[Data Cleansing & Outlier Treatment]
    B --> C[Feature Engineering: DTI, CUR, LTI, Enquiry Velocity]
    C --> D[Optimal Binning via Decision Tree / ChiMerge]
    D --> E[WoE Transformation]
    E --> F[IV-based Feature Selection]
    F --> G[Logistic Regression Training]
    G --> H[KS / AUC / Gini Validation]
    H --> I{Acceptance Threshold Met?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Scorecard Scaling: PDO + Offset]
    I -- No --> K[Refit with Engineered Features]
    K --> G
    J --> L[Out-of-Time Validation: PSI < 0.10]
    L --> M[Production Deployment]
    M --> N[Monthly PSI Monitoring]
    N --> O{PSI > 0.25?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Trigger Model Rebuild]
    O -- No --> N
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
