# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]

> **Q1.** Define **Information Value (IV)** and **Weight of Evidence (WoE)**. State the IV thresholds used to classify a feature's predictive power in a credit risk scoring model.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Weight of Evidence (WoE)** (1 Mark): WoE is the natural log of the ratio of the proportion of goods to the proportion of bads in a given bin of a feature: $\text{WoE}_i = \ln(\text{Dist}_{\text{Good},i} / \text{Dist}_{\text{Bad},i})$.
- **Information Value (IV)** (1 Mark): IV aggregates the predictive contribution of all bins: $\text{IV} = \sum (\text{Dist}_{\text{Good},i} - \text{Dist}_{\text{Bad},i}) \times \text{WoE}_i$.
- **IV Thresholds** (1 Mark): < 0.02 = Not predictive (drop), 0.02–0.1 = Weak, 0.1–0.3 = Medium, 0.3–0.5 = Strong, ≥ 0.5 = Suspicious (check leakage).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Pattern]

> **Q2.** What is the **KS Statistic** in credit scoring? What is the industry-acceptable minimum value for a retail scorecard?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition** (2 Marks): The Kolmogorov–Smirnov statistic measures the maximum vertical separation between the cumulative distribution functions of the model scores for the good and bad populations: $\text{KS} = \max \vert F_{\text{Good}}(x) - F_{\text{Bad}}(x) \vert$. A higher KS means the model discriminates better between defaulters and non-defaulters.
- **Acceptable Threshold** (1 Mark): **KS ≥ 0.40** is the industry standard minimum for retail scorecards. Below 0.30, the model is typically rejected for production.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Pattern – Module 3 Comprehensive]

> **Q (a) [7 Marks].** Explain the **end-to-end pipeline for implementing a credit risk scoring model** using logistic regression. List and briefly describe each stage from problem definition to deployment.

**Model Answer — Part (a):**
*(Mark allocation: 1 mark per stage × 6 stages = 6 marks + 1 mark for the diagram description)*

1. **Problem Definition (1M):** Define "default" (e.g., 90+ DPD), target window (12 months), population scope (new applications vs existing portfolio), and analytical objective (binary classification).
2. **Data Acquisition (1M):** Source from Core Banking, Credit Bureau (e.g., CIBIL/Experian), internal behaviour DB. Construct analysis-ready development sample with both good and bad labels.
3. **Data Preprocessing (1M):** Handle missing values (median imputation for numeric, mode for categorical), outlier capping (1st–99th percentile), and class imbalance treatment (typically 8 % bad rate, addressed via SMOTE or class weights).
4. **Feature Engineering & Binning (1M):** Compute derived ratios (DTI, CUR, LTI). Perform optimal binning (ChiMerge or decision-tree-based). Replace each bin with its WoE value.
5. **Feature Selection (1M):** Compute IV for each feature; retain features with 0.02 ≤ IV < 0.5. Eliminate multicollinearity via VIF < 5. Apply stepwise logistic regression (forward selection by AIC).
6. **Model Training, Validation & Deployment (1M):** Fit logistic regression on WoE-transformed features. Validate using OOS (k-fold) and OOT samples. Convert coefficients to scorecard points using PDO and Offset. Deploy to production with monthly PSI monitoring.
7. **Continuous Validation (Bonus 1M):** PSI < 0.10 = stable, 0.10–0.25 = investigate, > 0.25 = rebuild.

---

> **Q (b) [7 Marks].** For a feature with the following binning table, compute the **Information Value (IV)** and interpret the result. Comment on whether this feature should be retained.
>
> | Bin | Goods | Bads |
> |---|---:|---:|
> | A | 800 | 30 |
> | B | 600 | 70 |
> | C | 300 | 100 |
> | **Total** | **1,700** | **200** |

**Model Answer — Part (b):** *(7 marks: 1M tabulation + 1M per bin + 1M summation + 2M interpretation + 1M decision)*

**Step 1: Distribution Calculations (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Dist}_{\text{Good}} = \left\{\frac{800}{1700}, \frac{600}{1700}, \frac{300}{1700}\right\} = \{0.4706, 0.3529, 0.1765\}$$

$$\text{Dist}_{\text{Bad}} = \left\{\frac{30}{200}, \frac{70}{200}, \frac{100}{200}\right\} = \{0.1500, 0.3500, 0.5000\}$$

**Step 2: WoE per Bin (2 Marks):**

$$\text{WoE}_A = \ln(0.4706 / 0.1500) = \ln(3.1373) = 1.1437$$

$$\text{WoE}_B = \ln(0.3529 / 0.3500) = \ln(1.0083) = 0.0083$$

$$\text{WoE}_C = \ln(0.1765 / 0.5000) = \ln(0.3530) = -1.0415$$

**Step 3: Information Value (1 Mark):**

$$\text{IV}_A = (0.4706 - 0.1500) \times 1.1437 = 0.3206 \times 1.1437 = 0.3667$$

$$\text{IV}_B = (0.3529 - 0.3500) \times 0.0083 = 0.0029 \times 0.0083 \approx 0.0000$$

$$\text{IV}_C = (0.1765 - 0.5000) \times (-1.0415) = (-0.3235) \times (-1.0415) = 0.3369$$

$$\text{IV}_{\text{Total}} = 0.3667 + 0.0000 + 0.3369 = 0.7036$$

**Step 4: Interpretation & Decision (2 Marks):**
- An IV of **0.70 is suspiciously high** (≥ 0.5 threshold). While statistically "very strong", such a value in a real dataset almost always indicates **target leakage or over-fitting**, where the feature's value at application time may have been influenced by the target.
- **Decision:** Investigate for leakage. If verified safe, retain with caution and cap weight in the model. If leakage confirmed, drop the feature entirely.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice]

> **Q (a) [7 Marks].** Derive the **scorecard scaling formula** for converting logistic regression coefficients into points. Given PDO = 30, target score = 650 at odds 30:1, calculate the Factor and Offset.

**Model Answer — Part (a):** *(7 marks: 2M derivation + 1M Factor + 1M Offset + 1M application + 2M interpretation)*

**Derivation (2 Marks):**
The score is defined as a linear function of the natural log of the odds:

$$S = A + B \cdot \ln(\text{Odds})$$

Two conditions are imposed:
- At odds = 50:1, score = $S_0$
- A doubling of odds must reduce the score by PDO points

Solving these two equations yields:

$$B = \frac{\text{PDO}}{\ln 2}, \qquad A = S_0 - B \cdot \ln(\text{Odds}_0)$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$B = \text{Factor} = \frac{30}{\ln 2} = \frac{30}{0.6931} = 43.278$$

$$A = \text{Offset} = 650 - 43.278 \times \ln(30) = 650 - 43.278 \times 3.4012 = 650 - 147.20 = 502.80$$

**Application to a Bin (1 Mark):** If a feature has $\beta_j = -0.65$ and WoE$_b = 0.85$ with 5 total features in the model:

$$\text{Score}_b = -(-0.65)(0.85)(43.278) + \frac{502.80}{5} = 23.91 + 100.56 = 124.47 \text{ points}$$

**Interpretation (2 Marks):** Higher WoE = "good" behaviour bin = positive contribution = higher score. Lower WoE = "bad" behaviour = negative contribution = lower score, hence higher PD. The scorecard is the "translation layer" between statistical model and business user.

---

> **Q (b) [7 Marks].** Discuss the **handling of class imbalance** in credit scoring datasets. Compare at least three techniques with their advantages and limitations.

**Model Answer — Part (b):** *(7 marks: 1M framing + 2M per technique × 3 = 6M)*

**Framing (1 Mark):** Retail credit portfolios typically have a 2–8 % default rate. A naïve classifier predicting "no default" for everyone achieves 92–98 % accuracy but zero business value. The model must prioritise **recall on the minority (bad) class**.

**Technique 1: SMOTE — Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (2 Marks)**
- **Method:** Generates synthetic minority samples along line segments joining k-nearest neighbours of existing minority points.
- **Advantage:** Increases minority representation without exact duplication; reduces over-fitting relative to naïve oversampling.
- **Limitation:** Creates synthetic samples in feature space that may not correspond to real borrowers; can blur class boundaries if applied to noisy data.

**Technique 2: Undersampling (Random / Tomek Links) (2 Marks)**
- **Method:** Reduces majority (good) class to match minority size.
- **Advantage:** Faster training, simpler implementation.
- **Limitation:** Discards potentially valuable good-customer information; can under-fit if majority reduction is too aggressive.

**Technique 3: Cost-Sensitive Learning (2 Marks)**
- **Method:** Assigns asymmetric misclassification costs; e.g., $C_{\text{FN}} = 5 \times C_{\text{FP}}$ in loss-weighted logistic regression or scale_pos_weight in XGBoost.
- **Advantage:** Preserves the original data distribution; aligns mathematically with the Expected Loss framework.
- **Limitation:** Requires accurate cost estimates; may over-correct if cost ratios are mis-specified.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to evaluate on the ORIGINAL imbalanced test set.** If you resample the test set, your metrics (accuracy, F1) are inflated and meaningless. Always test on real distribution.
> 2. **Reporting accuracy as the primary metric.** A 95 % accuracy on a 5 % bad portfolio is worthless. Use **AUC-ROC, KS, F1, or PR-AUC** instead.
> 3. **Confusing IV and Gini.** They are not the same — IV is a feature-level metric, Gini is a model-level metric.
> 4. **Forgetting the monotonicity check** on WoE bins. Non-monotonic WoE indicates either too few bads per bin or sub-optimal cut-points.
> 5. **Using PCA or one-hot encoding before WoE transformation.** WoE requires ordered, binned categorical or continuous variables — raw numeric inputs break the transformation.
> 6. **Ignoring PSI in deployment.** A model with KS = 0.50 in development but PSI = 0.30 in production is **already failing** — score distribution has drifted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Print This Before Exam**

- ✅ **Credit Scoring = PD Ranking** — primary objective is rank-ordering, not absolute accuracy.
- ✅ **Pipeline = 6 Stages** — Problem → Data → Features → WoE/IV → Train → Validate → Deploy.
- ✅ **WoE = ln(Good% / Bad%)** — monotonically decreasing WoE = good binning.
- ✅ **IV Threshold = 0.5 is the leakage tripwire** — investigate, don't auto-accept.
- ✅ **Logistic PD = 1 / (1 + e^(−z))** with $z = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i \text{WoE}_i$.
- ✅ **Score Factor = PDO / ln 2** and **Offset = S₀ − Factor × ln(Odds₀)**.
- ✅ **KS ≥ 0.40** = retail-acceptable; KS < 0.30 = reject the model.
- ✅ **Gini = 2 × AUC − 1**; Gini ≥ 0.50 is desirable.
- ✅ **PSI thresholds: < 0.10 stable, 0.10–0.25 investigate, > 0.25 rebuild.**
- ✅ **Class imbalance ≠ solved by accuracy** — use F1 / AUC-ROC / PR-AUC.
- ✅ **Default horizon = 12 months** under Basel II IRB retail definition.
- ✅ **Expected Loss = PD × LGD × EAD** — the financial north star of every model.
- ✅ **Scorecard bins must be ≥ 5 % of population each** to ensure statistical stability.
- ✅ **WoE transformation requires binning first** — never feed raw continuous variables to logistic regression scorecard.
- ✅ **Logistic regression dominates regulation**; **XGBoost dominates accuracy** in fintech.
- ✅ **VIF < 5** for multicollinearity check among selected features.
- ✅ **Cost-sensitive threshold tuning > 0.5 default** when FN cost > FP cost.
- ✅ **Out-of-Time (OOT) validation is non-negotiable** — temporal leakage kills production models.
- ✅ **A scorecard with AUC > 0.95 in dev but 0.70 in OOT is over-fit** — retrain with regularisation.
- ✅ **Monitor: KS, Gini, PSI, default rate drift, approval rate drift** post-deployment.
- ✅ **Always convert WoE to points using per-feature bin scores, not single-feature contributions** in a multi-feature model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Algorithmic Trading - Basics of Trading Strategies, High-Frequency Trading, Algo-Bots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT742 - FINANCIAL DATA SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Algorithmic Trading - Basics of Trading Strategies, High-Frequency Trading, Algo-Bots # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Algorithmic Trading (Algo-Trading) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Algorithmic Trading* is the automated execution of trade orders in financial markets using pre-programmed computer instructions that account for variables such as **price**, **timing**, and **volume**. It leverages quantitative models, statistical inference, and machine learning algorithms to systematically identify and exploit trading opportunities in equities, derivatives, FX, and commodities markets. **Key Formal Attributes (as per KTU 2024 Scheme):** - **Automation Layer:** Rule-based or model-driven execution with **zero human discretion** at the point of order entry. - **Latency Profile:** Order-to-wire latency ranging from **microseconds (μs)** to **seconds (s)** depending on the strategy class. - **Signal Source:** Statistical, fundamental, technical, or sentiment-driven inputs. - **Decision Boundary:** Binary (Buy/Sell/Hold) or continuous (position sizing via fractional Kelly criterion). > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "The Robotic Stockbroker":** > Imagine a chef in a busy restaurant. Instead of personally taking orders, running to tables, and cooking, the chef installs a system of conveyor belts and robotic arms that follow a fixed recipe book. When a new order arrives, the robots automatically fetch ingredients, cook the dish, and deliver it — all without the chef lifting a finger. Algorithmic trading is this automation applied to financial markets. The "recipe book" is the trading strategy, the "robots" are the algorithms, and the "dishes" are buy/sell orders. This eliminates emotional bias (over-ordering or under-cooking), ensures speed (multiple dishes per second), and maintains consistency. --- ## 1.2 Trading Strategies > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > A *Trading Strategy* is a **rule-based, deterministic or probabilistic framework** that generates entry, exit, and position-sizing signals for financial instruments based on mathematical or statistical analysis of market data. **Strategic Taxonomy (Core KTU Module 4 Syllabus Categories):** | Strategy Class | Core Premise | Typical Holding Period | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Trend-Following** | "The trend is your friend" — momentum persistence | Days to Months | | **Mean Reversion** | Prices revert to long-term statistical mean | Minutes to Days | | **Statistical Arbitrage** | Cointegration/cointegration-based pairs trading | Seconds to Hours | | **Market Making** | Bid-ask spread capture via continuous quoting | Microseconds to Minutes | | **Event-Driven** | Exploits corporate actions, earnings, M&A | Hours to Days | | **High-Frequency Trading (HFT)** | Latency arbitrage and order-book microstructure exploitation | Microseconds to Seconds | --- ## 1.3 High-Frequency Trading (HFT) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > *High-Frequency Trading* is a subset of algorithmic trading characterized by **(i) sub-millisecond order placement**, **(ii) extremely short position-holding periods (often microseconds to seconds)**, and **(iii) extremely high message rates** (thousands of orders per second) with **minimal per-trade gross profit**, relying on cumulative edge at scale. **KTU-Relevant HFT Identity Markers:** - **Latency:** End-to-end round-trip latency typically **< 1 millisecond**. - **Order-to-Trade Ratio (OTR):** Frequently **> 100:1** (cancel-heavy quoting). - **Co-location:** Servers physically placed inside exchange data centers. - **Profit Per Trade:** Often **fractions of a cent**, monetized through volume. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "The Subway Sandwich vs. The Fine-Dining Tasting Menu":** > Algorithmic trading is like running a fine-dining restaurant where every dish takes time, care, and precision. HFT is the high-speed sandwich shop at a subway station — each sandwich (trade) earns only a thin margin, but the shop sells **millions per day**. The business doesn't need each sandwich to be profitable; it needs **velocity and scale** to make the cumulative margin astronomical. Likewise, an HFT firm might earn **$0.001 per share** but execute **billions of shares daily**. --- ## 1.4 Algo-Bots (Trading Robots) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > *Algo-Bots* (or *Trading Bots*) are **software agents** that autonomously monitor financial markets, apply a specific strategy, and submit orders to an exchange via APIs (FIX protocol, REST, WebSocket) — typically operating **24/5** across global sessions. **Bot Classification Taxonomy:** - **Signal Bots:** Only emit alerts; human executes manually. - **Semi-Automatic Bots:** Generate signals + suggest sizing; human approves. - **Fully Automatic Bots:** End-to-end execution; no human intervention. - **Hybrid AI-Bots:** Combine classical strategies with LLM-driven news/sentiment ingestion. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "The Self-Driving Car for Markets":** > A traditional trader is like a taxi driver who must personally steer every turn. An algo-bot is a **self-driving car**: it senses the environment (price feeds, news APIs), plans the route (strategy logic), and drives the order to the exchange — all while you sleep. Just as autonomous vehicles use sensors (cameras, LiDAR), algo-bots use **market data feeds, technical indicators, and NLP pipelines** as their perceptual sensors. --- ## 1.5 Foundational Distinction — Algo-Trading vs. HFT vs. Algo-Bots > [!NOTE] > **Hierarchical Relationship (Per KTU Module 4):** > All HFT is algorithmic trading, but **NOT** all algorithmic trading is HFT. > Algo-bots are the **delivery vehicles** (software containers) that execute either or both. $$ \text{Algo-Bots} \; \supset \; \text{Algo-Trading Strategies} \; \supset \; \text{High-Frequency Trading} $$ This nested-set notation implies HFT inherits algorithmic execution, and algo-bots are the universal runtime environment for both. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Trading Strategy — Five-Stage Operational Pipeline A KTU-examinable algorithmic strategy can be decomposed into five sequential stages: 1. **Data Ingestion Layer:** Real-time tick/OHLCV data streamed via WebSocket or FIX protocol. 2. **Signal Generation Layer:** Mathematical transformation of raw data into a discrete action (BUY, SELL, HOLD). 3. **Position Sizing Layer:** Allocation of capital based on risk parameters (Kelly criterion, volatility targeting). 4. **Execution Layer:** Order routing via Smart Order Router (SOR) to optimize fill price and minimize market impact. 5. **Risk & P&L Monitoring Layer:** Real-time tracking of exposure, drawdown, Sharpe ratio, and tail risk. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Every trading question you face will map to one of these five stages. Memorize this framework — it is the canonical "spine" of any Module 4 answer. --- ## 2.2 Mathematical Foundations — The Five Pillars ### Pillar 1: Simple Moving Average (SMA) — Trend Identification $$ \text{SMA}_n(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} P_{t-i} $$ Where: - $P_t$ = closing price at time $t$ - $n$ = look-back window (e.g., 20, 50, 200 periods) ### Pillar 2: Exponential Moving Average (EMA) — Weighted Trend $$ \text{EMA}_t \;=\; \alpha \cdot P_t \;+\; (1-\alpha)\cdot \text{EMA}_{t-1}, \quad \alpha \;=\; \frac{2}{n+1} $$ EMA assigns **exponentially decaying weights**, giving more importance to recent prices. Used in MACD, PPO, and adaptive momentum models. ### Pillar 3: Relative Strength Index (RSI) — Mean Reversion Gauge $$ \text{RSI} \;=\; 100 \;-\; \frac{100}{1 + \text{RS}}, \quad \text{RS} \;=\; \frac{\overline{\text{Gain}}_n}{\overline{\text{Loss}}_n} $$ - RSI > 70 → **Overbought** (potential SELL signal) - RSI < 30 → **Oversold** (potential BUY signal) ### Pillar 4: Sharpe Ratio — Risk-Adjusted Return $$ S \;=\; \frac{\mathbb{E}[R_p - R_f]}{\sigma_p} $$ Where $R_p$ = portfolio return, $R_f$ = risk-free rate, $\sigma_p$ = return standard deviation. - $S > 1$ → Acceptable performance - $S > 2$ → Strong performance - $S > 3$ → Exceptional (typical of elite quant funds) ### Pillar 5: Kelly Criterion — Optimal Position Sizing $$ f^\star \;=\; \frac{p}{a} \;-\; \frac{1-p}{b} \;=\; \frac{p \cdot b - q}{b} $$ Where: - $p$ = probability of winning trade - $q = 1 - p$ - $a$ = loss ratio (loss magnitude) - $b$ = win ratio (gain magnitude) --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Boundary / Threshold | Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $SMA_n(t) = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} P_{t-i}$ | $P_t$ = Price; $n$ = Window | $n \in [5, 200]$ | Trend baseline | | 2 | $EMA_t = \alpha P_t + (1-\alpha)EMA_{t-1}$ | $\alpha = \tfrac{2}{n+1}$ | $\alpha \in (0,1)$ | Reactive trend | | 3 | $RSI = 100 - \tfrac{100}{1+RS}$ | $RS = \tfrac{\bar{G}}{\bar{L}}$ | Overbought $> 70$; Oversold $< 30$ | Reversal | | 4 | $S = \tfrac{\mathbb{E}[R_p - R_f]}{\sigma_p}$ | $R_f$ = risk-free | $S > 1$ acceptable | Performance | | 5 | $f^\star = \tfrac{p}{a} - \tfrac{1-p}{b}$ | $p$ = win prob | $0 < f^\star < 1$ | Position size | | 6 | $\text{MACD} = EMA_{12} - EMA_{26}$ | Standard 12/26/9 | Crossover $\Rightarrow$ signal | Momentum | | 7 | $\text{Log Return} \; r_t = \ln(P_t/P_{t-1})$ | Continuous | $r_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$ | Statistical arb | | 8 | $\text{P\&L} = (P_{exit} - P_{entry}) \cdot Q - \text{Fees}$ | $Q$ = quantity | Net of slippage | Trade accounting | | 9 | $\text{Holding Period} \; T = t_{exit} - t_{entry}$ | In seconds | $T < 1s$ ⇒ HFT regime | Strategy classification | | 10 | $\text{Latency} \; L = t_{ack} - t_{submit}$ | Round-trip | $L < 1ms$ ⇒ HFT | Co-location metric | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Sharpe Ratio** with **Sortino Ratio**. Sharpe uses total volatility ($\sigma_p$); Sortino uses only downside deviation. For KTU exams, default to **Sharpe** unless the question explicitly states "downside deviation." --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Risk Management:** Sharpe and Sortino ratios are used by **BlackRock, Two Sigma**, and pension funds for capital allocation. - **HFT Infrastructure:** Engineers deploy **FPGA-accelerated NIC cards** to achieve sub-microsecond tick-to-trade latency. - **Algo-Bot APIs:** Modern retail platforms (Alpaca, Binance, Interactive Brokers) expose REST/WebSocket APIs enabling bot deployment in **Python, C++, Rust**. - **Regulatory Compliance:** SEBI (India) and SEC (USA) mandate **Market Access Rule** compliance — every algo must have pre-trade risk checks (max order size, price collars, kill switches). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Sharpe Ratio from First Principles **Objective:** Quantify the excess return per unit of total risk. ### Step 1 — Define Raw Return The simple return on a portfolio over one period is: $$ R_p \;=\; \frac{V_{t} - V_{t-1}}{V_{t-1}} $$ where $V_t$ is the portfolio value at time $t$. ### Step 2 — Compute Excess Return Subtract the risk-free rate (proxy: short-term Treasury yield, e.g., 3-month T-Bill rate): $$ R_{excess} \;=\; R_p - R_f $$ This isolates the *value-added* by the trading strategy, removing the compensation any investor could earn risk-free. ### Step 3 — Take Expected Value Across $N$ periods, the average excess return is: $$ \mathbb{E}[R_{excess}] \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(R_{p,i} - R_f) $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Standard Deviation of Excess Returns $$ \sigma_p \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left((R_{p,i} - R_f) - \mathbb{E}[R_{excess}]\right)^2} $$ ### Step 5 — Form the Sharpe Ratio $$ S \;=\; \frac{\mathbb{E}[R_{excess}]}{\sigma_p} \;=\; \frac{\mathbb{E}[R_p - R_f]}{\sigma_p} $$ This is the dimensionless reward-to-variability ratio. **Interpretation:** A Sharpe of 2.0 means the strategy earns 2 units of excess return for every 1 unit of volatility. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Moving Average Crossover Signal Logic **Objective:** Generate a discrete BUY/SELL signal from two EMAs. ### Step 1 — Compute Fast and Slow EMAs $$ \text{EMA}_{fast}(t) \;=\; \alpha_f P_t + (1-\alpha_f)\text{EMA}_{fast}(t-1) $$ $$ \text{EMA}_{slow}(t) \;=\; \alpha_s P_t + (1-\alpha_s)\text{EMA}_{slow}(t-1) $$ with $\alpha_f > \alpha_s$ (e.g., $\alpha_f = 2/13$, $\alpha_s = 2/27$). ### Step 2 — Compute the MACD Line $$ \text{MACD}(t) \;=\; \text{EMA}_{fast}(t) - \text{EMA}_{slow}(t) $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Signal Line $$ \text{Signal}(t) \;=\; \text{EMA}_9(\text{MACD}(t)) $$ A 9-period EMA of MACD. ### Step 4 — Generate Cross-Signal $$ \text{Action}(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{BUY} & \text{if MACD}(t) > \text{Signal}(t) \;\text{and}\; \text{MACD}(t-1) \le \text{Signal}(t-1) \\ \text{SELL} & \text{if MACD}(t) < \text{Signal}(t) \;\text{and}\; \text{MACD}(t-1) \ge \text{Signal}(t-1) \\ \text{HOLD} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Interpretation:** A BUY signal is emitted at the moment the MACD line crosses **above** the signal line — i.e., momentum turning bullish. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Kelly Criterion for Binary Outcomes **Objective:** Maximize the long-run logarithmic growth of capital. ### Step 1 — Setup the Game Consider a series of bets where: - Win probability = $p$ → gain fraction $b$ of bankroll - Loss probability = $q = 1-p$ → lose fraction $a$ of bankroll ### Step 2 — Expected Logarithmic Growth Rate Let $G(f)$ be the expected log-growth as a function of bet fraction $f$: $$ G(f) \;=\; p \ln(1 + bf) + q \ln(1 - af) $$ ### Step 3 — Optimize by Setting $dG/df = 0$ $$ \frac{dG}{df} \;=\; \frac{pb}{1+bf} - \frac{qa}{1-af} \;=\; 0 $$ ### Step 4 — Solve Algebraically $$ \frac{pb}{1+bf} \;=\; \frac{qa}{1-af} $$ $$ pb(1-af) \;=\; qa(1+bf) $$ $$ pb - pbaf \;=\; qa + qabf $$ $$ pb - qa \;=\; f(pba + qab) \;=\; fab(p + q) \;=\; fab $$ $$ f^\star \;=\; \frac{pb - qa}{ab} $$ ### Step 5 — Express in Win-Probability Form Assuming symmetric gain/loss ($a = b = 1$): $$ f^\star \;=\; p - q \;=\; 2p - 1 $$ **Example:** If your strategy has a 60% win rate with equal win/loss magnitudes: $$ f^\star = 0.60 - 0.40 = 0.20 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Bet 20\% of bankroll per trade} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Trend-Following | Mean Reversion | Statistical Arbitrage | HFT | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Holding Period** | Days–Weeks | Minutes–Days | Seconds–Hours | Microseconds–Seconds | | **Win Rate** | 35–45% | 55–70% | 51–55% | 50.1–51% | | **Avg Win/Loss Ratio** | 2:1 to 4:1 | 0.8:1 to 1.2:1 | 1:1 to 1.5:1 | 1.001:1 to 1.01:1 | | **Sharpe (Typical)** | 0.8–1.5 | 1.2–2.0 | 1.5–3.0 | 3.0–10.0+ | | **Data Frequency** | EOD, 1h | 1m, 5m | 1s, tick | Tick, L2 order book | | **Signal Math** | EMA, MACD | RSI, Bollinger | Cointegration | Microprice, queue position | | **Risk Control** | Stop-loss | Mean reversion bound | Z-score entry | Inventory limit | | **Typical Capital** | High | Medium | High | Very High + infra | --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — RSI Calculation **Given:** 14-day price series; compute RSI for the last close. ### Step 1 — Compute Daily Price Changes $$ \Delta P_t \;=\; P_t - P_{t-1} $$ ### Step 2 — Separate Gains and Losses $$ \text{Gain}_t = \begin{cases} \Delta P_t & \text{if } \Delta P_t > 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}, \quad \text{Loss}_t = \begin{cases} -\Delta P_t & \text{if } \Delta P_t < 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute 14-Period Averages $$ \overline{\text{Gain}}_{14} \;=\; \frac{1}{14}\sum_{t=1}^{14}\text{Gain}_t, \quad \overline{\text{Loss}}_{14} \;=\; \frac{1}{14}\sum_{t=1}^{14}\text{Loss}_t $$ ### Step 4 — Compute RS and RSI $$ RS \;=\; \frac{\overline{\text{Gain}}_{14}}{\overline{\text{Loss}}_{14}}, \quad \text{RSI} \;=\; 100 - \frac{100}{1 + RS} $$ **Numerical Illustration:** If average gain = $0.85$ and average loss = $0.45$: $$ RS = \frac{0.85}{0.45} = 1.889, \quad \text{RSI} = 100 - \frac{100}{2.889} = 100 - 34.62 = 65.38 $$ Since $65.38 < 70$, the asset is **not overbought** — no SELL signal. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Algorithmic Trading System Architecture The following block diagram maps the five operational stages of an institutional-grade algorithmic trading system, from market data ingestion to P&L attribution. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Market Data Feed
WebSocket and FIX] --> B[Tick Data Normalizer
OHLCV Aggregator] B --> C[Signal Engine
Indicator Library] C --> D{Strategy Logic
BUY SELL HOLD} D -- BUY --> E[Position Sizer
Kelly and Vol Target] D -- SELL --> F[Order Manager
Smart Order Router] D -- HOLD --> G[Risk Monitor
Drawdown and Exposure] E --> F F --> H[Exchange Matching Engine
NSE BSE NYSE] H --> I[Fill Confirmation
Trade Ticket] I --> J[P and L Attribution
Real Time Dashboard] G --> J J --> K[Risk Kill Switch
Circuit Breaker] K --> F style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 style H fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 style J fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2 ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - **Blue node (A):** Live market data ingress — the "eyes" of the system. - **Yellow node (C):** The mathematical brain where indicators (EMA, RSI, MACD) are computed. - **Red node (D):** The decision diamond — every path begins here. - **Green node (H):** The exchange — final execution venue. - **Purple node (J):** Continuous performance accounting loop. --- ## 4.2 High-Frequency Trading (HFT) Micro-Architecture HFT requires specialized hardware acceleration to achieve sub-millisecond latencies. The following diagram illustrates the latency-critical path. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Co-located Server
Inside Exchange Data Center] --> A2[Kernel Bypass NIC
DPDK or Solarflare] A2 --> A3[FPGA Pre-processor
Hardware Order Book Builder] A3 --> A4[Strategy Logic in FPGA
Sub Microsecond Decision] A4 --> A5{Order or Cancel} A5 -- Order --> A6[Direct Exchange Gateway
UDP Multicast] A5 -- Cancel --> A7[Cancel via Outbound Queue] A6 --> A8[Matching Engine Fill
Microsecond Ack] A8 --> A9[Market Data Response
UDP Unicast] A9 --> A4 style A1 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#c9184a style A3 fill:#c9e4de,stroke:#52b788 style A4 fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#0077b6 style A8 fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#f3722c ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - **Pink node (A1):** Physical proximity to exchange — co-location eliminates ~70% of network latency. - **Green node (A3):** FPGA offloads the most latency-sensitive parsing, achieving **< 100 ns** parsing overhead. - **Blue node (A4):** Decision logic runs in hardware, not software. - **Orange node (A8):** Matching engine acknowledgment — total round-trip often **< 5 microseconds** for top-tier HFT firms. --- ## 4.3 Algo-Bot Functional Architecture (Software Layer) This diagram depicts the internal modular architecture of a typical algo-bot, separating concerns for maintainability and audit. ```mermaid flowchart LR B1[Configuration Module
Strategy Parameters] --> B2[Data Connector
API Client] B2 --> B3[Feature Engine
Indicator Pipeline] B3 --> B4[Strategy Core
Rule Evaluator] B4 --> B5[Risk Gate
Pre Trade Checks] B5 -- Pass --> B6[Order Executor
Broker API] B5 -- Fail --> B7[Alert Logger
Telegram and Email] B6 --> B8[Position Tracker
In Memory State] B8 --> B4 B8 --> B9[P and L Reporter
Database Sink] B9 --> B10[Dashboard UI
Streamlit and Grafana] style B1 fill:#e0fbfc,stroke:#3d5a80 style B4 fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#e07a5f style B5 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f style B6 fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#5a189a style B10 fill:#ffc6ff,stroke:#9d4edd ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - **Sky-blue node (B1):** Hot-reloadable config — supports parameter sweeps and live tuning. - **Orange node (B4):** The "heart" — pure strategy logic, no I/O concerns. - **Green node (B5):** Risk gate enforces **max order size, price collars, daily loss limits** before any order leaves the bot. - **Purple node (B6):** Order executor communicates via REST/WebSocket with broker. - **Pink node (B10):** Real-time visualization layer for human oversight. --- ## 4.4 Strategy Decision Tree — Trend vs. Mean Reversion Classifier A practical algo-bot often uses a meta-classifier to decide *which* sub-strategy to deploy. ```mermaid flowchart TD C1[New Tick Arrives] --> C2{ADX Greater Than 25} C2 -- Yes --> C3{Trending Market
Deploy Trend Strategy} C2 -- No --> C4{Range Bound
Deploy Mean Reversion} C3 --> C5[Apply EMA Crossover
and MACD] C4 --> C6[Apply RSI and Bollinger] C5 --> C7[Compute Signal] C6 --> C7 C7 --> C8{Confidence Above Threshold} C8 -- Yes --> C9[Submit Order] C8 -- No --> C10[Skip and Log] style C2 fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c9184a style C3 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f style C4 fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#0077b6 style C9 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#f3722c ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** The **ADX (Average Directional Index)** is the gatekeeper. When ADX > 25, the market exhibits strong directional movement → trend strategy. When ADX < 25, prices oscillate within a range → mean reversion. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]** > **Q: Differentiate between Algorithmic Trading and High-Frequency Trading. List any two distinguishing parameters.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Algorithmic Trading | High-Frequency Trading | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Latency** | Seconds to minutes | Microseconds to milliseconds | | **Holding Period** | Minutes to months | Microseconds to seconds | | **Order Rate** | Hundreds/day | Thousands/second | | **Capital Intensity** | Low to medium | Very high (infra + co-location) | | **Strategy Diversity** | Wide (trend, arb, event) | Narrow (market-making, latency arb) | | **Infrastructure** | Standard servers | FPGA, co-location, kernel bypass | **Key Distinction:** *All HFT is algorithmic trading, but not all algorithmic trading is HFT.* HFT is a specialized subset characterized by extreme latency optimization and short-duration positions. > [!NOTE] > **Marks Allocation Tip (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):** 1 mark for each differentiating parameter with a short justification. 1 mark for the "subset" relationship statement. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]** > **Q: What is an Algo-Bot? List its four core functional modules.** **Model Answer:** An **Algo-Bot** (Algorithmic Trading Robot) is a software agent that autonomously executes trading decisions in financial markets based on pre-programmed rules, without continuous human intervention. **Four Core Functional Modules:** 1. **Data Acquisition Module** — Connects to exchange APIs to ingest real-time price/volume feeds. 2. **Signal Generation Module** — Applies technical/fundamental indicators to produce BUY/SELL/HOLD decisions. 3. **Risk Management Module** — Enforces pre-trade checks: position limits, max drawdown, kill switches. 4. **Execution Module** — Routes orders to brokers/exchanges via FIX/REST/WebSocket protocols. > [!NOTE] > **Marks Allocation Tip:** 1 mark for definition + 0.5 × 4 = 2 marks for listing modules with one-line explanations of each. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Mandate:** Per KTU ESE 2024 Scheme, students may answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both are provided below as independent 14-mark modules. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Algorithmic Trading Strategies Deep Dive **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]** > **Q (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the Moving Average Crossover strategy with its mathematical formulation. Describe the conditions for generating BUY and SELL signals. How is the EMA different from SMA, and why is EMA preferred for short-term strategies?** > **Q (b) [7 Marks]: A trading strategy has a 55% win rate with an average win of ₹150 and average loss of ₹100 per trade. Compute the optimal Kelly fraction. If the trader uses half-Kelly for safety, how much capital should be allocated per trade from a ₹10,00,000 bankroll?** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — SMA Formulation:** The Simple Moving Average over $n$ periods is: $$ \text{SMA}_n(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} P_{t-i} $$ **Step 2 — EMA Formulation:** The Exponential Moving Average applies exponentially decaying weights: $$ \text{EMA}_t \;=\; \alpha P_t + (1-\alpha)\text{EMA}_{t-1}, \quad \alpha = \frac{2}{n+1} $$ **Step 3 — Crossover Logic:** A two-MA crossover strategy uses a fast MA (e.g., 9-period EMA) and slow MA (e.g., 21-period EMA): - **BUY Signal:** When the fast MA crosses **above** the slow MA → bullish momentum confirmed. - **SELL Signal:** When the fast MA crosses **below** the slow MA → bearish momentum confirmed. $$ \text{Signal}(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{BUY} & \text{if } EMA_f(t) > EMA_s(t) \;\text{and}\; EMA_f(t-1) \le EMA_s(t-1) \\ \text{SELL} & \text{if } EMA_f(t) < EMA_s(t) \;\text{and}\; EMA_f(t-1) \ge EMA_s(t-1) \\ \text{HOLD} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step 4 — EMA vs. SMA Comparison:** | Property | SMA | EMA | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Weighting | Equal for all periods | Exponentially decreasing | | Responsiveness | Lagging (slow) | Faster (recent-weighted) | | Noise Sensitivity | Lower (smoothed) | Higher (reactive) | | Preferred For | Long-term trend (50, 200 DMA) | Short-term momentum (9, 21 EMA) | **Step 5 — Why EMA for Short-Term:** EMA's higher weighting on recent prices makes it more **responsive to new information**. For short-term strategies where reaction speed is critical, this responsiveness is more valuable than SMA's noise-filtering smoothing. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Given:** - Win probability: $p = 0.55$ - Loss probability: $q = 0.45$ - Average win: $b = 150$ (gain ratio relative to loss unit) - Average loss: $a = 100$ (loss unit) **Step 1 — Apply Kelly Formula:** $$ f^\star \;=\; \frac{pb - qa}{ab} \;=\; \frac{(0.55)(150) - (0.45)(100)}{(100)(150)} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Numerator:** $$ (0.55)(150) = 82.50, \quad (0.45)(100) = 45.00 $$ $$ 82.50 - 45.00 = 37.50 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Denominator:** $$ (100)(150) = 15{,}000 $$ **Step 4 — Compute Full Kelly:** $$ f^\star = \frac{37.50}{15{,}000} = 0.0025 = 0.25\% $$ **Step 5 — Half-Kelly for Safety:** $$ f_{\text{half}} = \frac{f^\star}{2} = \frac{0.0025}{2} = 0.00125 = 0.125\% $$ **Step 6 — Capital Allocation per Trade:** $$ \text{Capital per trade} = 0.125\% \times ₹10{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}250 $$ **Final Answer:** The trader should allocate **₹1,250 per trade** using half-Kelly, yielding 0.125% of the bankroll per position. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Part (b):** > - **Wrong units:** Mixing $p$ and $q$ as percentages vs. decimals. Always use decimals (0.55, not 55). > - **Kelly vs. Half-Kelly confusion:** If the question asks for "full Kelly," do not divide by 2. Read carefully. > - **Final answer units:** Students often write "0.125%" without converting to ₹. Always provide the monetary allocation. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — High-Frequency Trading & Algo-Bots **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]** > **Q (a) [7 Marks]: Describe the defining characteristics of High-Frequency Trading (HFT). Explain the role of co-location and FPGA acceleration in achieving sub-millisecond latency. Why are HFT strategies called "latency-sensitive arbitrage"?** > **Q (b) [7 Marks]: Explain the architecture of a typical Algo-Bot with reference to its key modules. Discuss the importance of the Risk Gate (Pre-Trade Risk Check) module with at least three example checks enforced by SEBI's Market Access Rule.** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Defining Characteristics of HFT (5 pillars):** 1. **Sub-millisecond Latency:** Order-to-wire latency in microseconds (typically **< 1 ms**, often **< 100 μs** for top firms). 2. **High Message Rate:** Thousands of order submissions, amendments, and cancellations per second. 3. **Short Holding Period:** Positions held from **microseconds to seconds** (never days). 4. **Co-location Dependency:** Servers physically placed inside the exchange's data center to minimize network propagation delay. 5. **Low Gross Margin Per Trade:** Profit per trade is tiny (fractions of a cent), but multiplied by astronomical volume. **Step 2 — Role of Co-location:** - The speed of light in fiber optic cable is approximately **~200,000 km/s**. - A round-trip from Mumbai to Singapore (~5,000 km) introduces a one-way delay of ~25 ms — catastrophic for HFT. - Co-location places the trader's server **adjacent to the exchange matching engine**, reducing one-way latency to **< 1 μs**. - Result: **~70% reduction in network-induced latency** vs. remote hosting. **Step 3 — Role of FPGA Acceleration:** - **FPGA** (Field-Programmable Gate Array) is a hardware chip that executes deterministic logic in nanoseconds, bypassing the OS kernel. - Software-based parsing of market data incurs ~1–10 μs of OS jitter. - FPGA-based parsing reduces this to **< 100 ns**, with deterministic tail latency. - FPGAs are used for **hardware order book building, signal generation, and order submission** — the most latency-critical operations. **Step 4 — Why "Latency-Sensitive Arbitrage":** HFT strategies exploit **microsecond-scale price discrepancies** between correlated instruments (e.g., SPY futures vs. SPY ETF, or NIFTY spot vs. NIFTY futures). The arbitrage opportunity exists for **only a few microseconds** before other HFTs or market makers close it. Therefore, **the firm with the lowest latency captures the alpha**. This is why HFT is fundamentally a **latency race** — hence the term "latency-sensitive arbitrage." #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Algo-Bot Architecture Overview:** A typical algo-bot is organized into modular components for **separation of concerns, auditability, and hot-reloadability**. Reference the architecture diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes for the full visual. **Core Modules:** 1. **Configuration Module** — Strategy parameters, ticker symbols, time windows. 2. **Data Connector** — API client for market data ingestion (WebSocket/FIX). 3. **Feature Engine** — Indicator library (EMA, RSI, MACD, Bollinger Bands). 4. **Strategy Core** — Rule evaluator emitting BUY/SELL/HOLD signals. 5. **Risk Gate (Pre-Trade Risk Check)** — Mandatory validation layer before order submission. 6. **Order Executor** — Broker/exchange API client for order routing. 7. **Position Tracker** — In-memory state of open positions and P&L. 8. **P&L Reporter** — Database sink + dashboard UI. **Step 2 — Importance of the Risk Gate:** The Risk Gate is the **last line of defense** before an order is transmitted to the exchange. A malfunctioning bot without a robust risk gate can issue runaway orders, causing catastrophic financial loss (e.g., the 2010 Flash Crash, Knight Capital's $440M loss in 2012). **Step 3 — SEBI Market Access Rule — Three Mandatory Pre-Trade Checks:** 1. **Maximum Order Quantity Check:** $$ Q_{order} \le Q_{max} $$ Prevents oversized orders that could exceed the trader's intended exposure. 2. **Price Collar Check:** $$ P_{order} \in [P_{lower}, P_{upper}], \quad P_{upper} = P_{ref}(1 + \delta), \quad P_{lower} = P_{ref}(1 - \delta) $$ where $\delta$ is the permissible deviation (typically 1–5%). Prevents "fat-finger" errors (e.g., buying at ₹10,000 instead of ₹1000). 3. **Daily Loss Limit Check:** $$ \text{Loss}_{\text{today}} \le \text{Loss}_{\text{max}} $$ When cumulative intraday loss exceeds the threshold, the kill switch disables the bot automatically. SEBI mandates a **hard kill switch** accessible to the exchange. **Step 4 — Additional Best-Practice Checks:** - **Max open position check** (no over-leveraging) - **Self-trade prevention** (no wash trades against own orders) - **Duplicate order check** (prevents accidental multi-submission) - **Trading hours check** (no orders outside exchange sessions) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Part (b):** > - **Confusing pre-trade vs. post-trade risk:** Pre-trade = before order leaves bot (Risk Gate). Post-trade = after fill (P&L monitoring). KTU expects clear distinction. > - **Omitting SEBI specifics:** Generic "risk check" answers score lower than citing SEBI Market Access Rule with named parameters. > - **Architecture without examples:** Don't just list modules — explain the *purpose* and *flow* between them. --- ## 5.3 KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Warnings > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Module 4 — Algorithmic Trading:** > > 1. **Confusing terms:** Students interchange "Algo-Trading," "HFT," and "Algo-Bots" without acknowledging the hierarchical relationship. > 2. **Missing mathematical depth:** A question on EMA crossover without the $\alpha = 2/(n+1)$ derivation will score **at most 3 out of 7** marks. > 3. **No real-world examples:** Naming "Citadel" or "Renaissance Technologies" as HFT firms adds credibility to your answer. > 4. **Ignoring risk management:** Every algorithmic system answer should explicitly mention the Risk Gate and kill switch. > 5. **Calculation errors in Kelly Criterion:** Verify your $p$ and $q$ sum to 1, and your $a$, $b$ magnitudes are correctly oriented. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4):** - **Hierarchical Relationship:** Algo-Bots ⊃ Algo-Trading ⊃ HFT — memorize the nested-set notation. - **Five Stages of an Algo Strategy:** Data Ingestion → Signal Generation → Position Sizing → Execution → Risk/P&L Monitoring. - **Sharpe Ratio** $S = \mathbb{E}[R_p - R_f]/\sigma_p$ — benchmark: $S > 1$ acceptable, $S > 2$ strong, $S > 3$ elite. - **Kelly Criterion** $f^\star = (pb - qa)/(ab)$ — always default to **half-Kelly** in practice to reduce ruin probability. - **EMA > SMA** for short-term responsiveness; **SMA > EMA** for long-term smoothing. - **RSI Thresholds:** Overbought > 70 → SELL signal; Oversold < 30 → BUY signal. - **MACD = EMA₁₂ − EMA₂₆; Signal = EMA₉(MACD).** Crossover = momentum signal. - **HFT Identity:** Sub-ms latency, high message rate, short holding period, co-location, FPGA, low per-trade margin. - **Algo-Bot Modules:** Configuration, Data Connector, Feature Engine, Strategy Core, Risk Gate, Executor, Position Tracker, P&L Reporter. - **Pre-Trade Risk Checks:** Max order size, price collar, daily loss limit, kill switch, self-trade prevention. - **SEBI Compliance:** Market Access Rule mandates pre-trade risk checks for every algo accessing Indian markets. - **Strategy Classes:** Trend-following, Mean Reversion, Statistical Arbitrage, Market Making, Event-Driven, HFT. - **Real-World Firms:** Renaissance Technologies, Citadel Securities, Two Sigma, Jane Street, Jump Trading. - **Co-location math:** Light in fiber ~200,000 km/s → 5,000 km = 25 ms one-way → co-location reduces to < 1 μs. - **FPGA advantage:** Sub-100 ns parsing vs. 1–10 μs OS jitter — eliminates software-induced latency variance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For Module 4 questions, structure every answer using the **5-stage framework** (Data → Signal → Sizing → Execution → Risk). This shows the examiner you understand the *system*, not just isolated formulas. Always cite SEBI regulations when discussing Indian algo-trading to score maximum marks on application questions.

topics.title: Financial Portfolio Theory - Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT), Efficient Frontier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT742 - FINANCIAL DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Financial Portfolio Theory - Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT), Efficient Frontier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)** is a mathematical framework developed by **Harry M. Markowitz (1952)** — earning him the **Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences in 1990** — that constructs optimal investment portfolios by quantitatively balancing **expected return** against **risk**, with the central thesis that an asset's standalone risk is less important than its **marginal contribution to overall portfolio risk**, contingent on its **correlation structure** with other portfolio constituents.

The theory rests on three foundational pillars:
1. **Portfolio Expected Return** — the probability-weighted average of constituent asset returns.
2. **Portfolio Risk (Variance / Standard Deviation)** — a function of individual asset variances **and** pairwise covariances.
3. **Diversification Benefit** — the reduction of idiosyncratic (firm-specific) risk through the combination of imperfectly correlated assets.

### 1.2 The Efficient Frontier (Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Efficient Frontier** is the geometric locus of all *mean-variance optimal* portfolios lying in the $\mathbb{R}^2$ space defined by portfolio risk ($\sigma_p$) on the abscissa and expected return $E(R_p)$ on the ordinate, that simultaneously satisfy: **(i) no other portfolio offers higher expected return for the same risk level**, and **(ii) no other portfolio exhibits lower risk for the same expected return**. Portfolios lying *below* the frontier are **inefficient** (sub-optimal); portfolios *above* the frontier are mathematically **unattainable** under the given asset universe.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **road trip from Kochi to Delhi**. You have three possible vehicles: a **fuel-efficient but slow** Maruti sedan, a **fast but fuel-thirsty** Royal Enfield, and a **balanced** Toyota Innova.

- If you care only about *speed* → choose the Enfield (high return, high risk — high fuel stops and breakdowns).
- If you care only about *fuel economy* → choose the Maruti (low return, low risk — boring, slow journey).
- But Markowitz says: *why not blend them?* By allocating, say, **50% Innova, 30% Enfield, 20% Maruti**, you smooth the ride. The vehicles don't break down at the *same* potholes (low correlation), and you reach faster than the Maruti while burning less fuel than a solo Enfield.

This "smoothing" is **diversification** — and the **best blends**, plotted as a curve, form the **Efficient Frontier**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used Throughout MPT:**
> - **Risk-free rate ($R_f$):** Typically proxied by **T-Bill yield** (e.g., 6-month G-Sec in India ≈ 6.5%–7% as of 2024).
> - **Excess Return / Risk Premium:** $E(R_i) - R_f$.
> - **Sharpe Ratio:** Denoted $S_p$ — risk-adjusted performance measure.

### 1.4 The Two Asset Classes in MPT

| Asset Class | Description | KTU Examination Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **Risky Assets** | Stocks, corporate bonds, commodities, REITs | Very High (≈ 70% of numericals) |
| **Risk-Free Asset** | Government Treasury Bills (default-free) | High (Capital Market Line derivations) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Assumptions of MPT (Frequently Asked in Part A)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board expects verbatim recall of these assumptions.**

1. **Rational Investors** — they prefer more wealth to less and are risk-averse.
2. **Single-Period Horizon** — all decisions are made for a single future time horizon $t=1$.
3. **Normally Distributed Returns** — $R_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i, \sigma_i^2)$ so mean and variance fully characterize the distribution.
4. **Homogeneous Expectations** — all investors use identical input estimates $(\mu_i, \sigma_i, \rho_{ij})$.
5. **Frictionless Markets** — no taxes, transaction costs, or liquidity constraints.
6. **Assets are Infinitely Divisible** — weights $w_i$ can take any real value (including short-selling $w_i < 0$).

### 2.2 The Core Concept: Why Diversification Works

The mathematical "miracle" of MPT is this: for a portfolio of $N$ equally-weighted assets with *average* pairwise correlation $\bar{\rho}$,

$$\sigma_p^2 = \frac{1}{N}\bar{\sigma}^2 + \frac{N-1}{N}\bar{\rho}\bar{\sigma}^2$$

- As $N \to \infty$, the **first term (idiosyncratic risk) → 0**.
- Only the **systematic risk** (the $\bar{\rho}$ term, driven by market correlation) survives.
- **Conclusion:** Diversification eliminates *unsystematic* risk but *cannot* eliminate systematic (market) risk.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your KTU exam quick-reference. Memorize the *formulas* column exactly.**

| $\#$ | Concept | Mathematical Formulation | Notes / Conditions |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Expected Return of Asset $i$ | $E(R_i) = \sum_{s=1}^{S} p_s \cdot R_{i,s}$ | $p_s$ = probability of state $s$ |
| 2 | Portfolio Expected Return ($N$ assets) | $E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot E(R_i)$ | Weights satisfy $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| 3 | Variance of Single Asset | $\sigma_i^2 = \sum_{s=1}^{S} p_s [R_{i,s} - E(R_i)]^2$ | Non-negative scalar |
| 4 | Covariance (Pair of Assets) | $\mathrm{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = \sigma_{ij} = \rho_{ij} \cdot \sigma_i \cdot \sigma_j$ | Bounded: $\sigma_{ij}^2 \leq \sigma_i^2 \sigma_j^2$ |
| 5 | Correlation Coefficient | $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ij}}{\sigma_i \cdot \sigma_j}$ | Range: $[-1, +1]$ |
| 6 | **2-Asset Portfolio Variance** | $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2w_1 w_2 \sigma_{12}$ | The **most-tested formula** in KTU |
| 7 | **$N$-Asset Portfolio Variance** | $\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_i w_j \sigma_{ij}$ | Matrix form: $\mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ |
| 8 | Global Minimum Variance Portfolio (2-Asset) | $w_1^{\mathrm{GMV}} = \dfrac{\sigma_2^2 - \sigma_{12}}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\sigma_{12}}$ | Set $\dfrac{d\sigma_p^2}{dw_1} = 0$ |
| 9 | Tangent (Optimal Risky) Portfolio | $\mathbf{w}^{\star} \propto \Sigma^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu} - R_f \mathbf{1})$ | Maximize Sharpe Ratio |
| 10 | Sharpe Ratio | $S_p = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Higher $S_p \Rightarrow$ better risk-adjusted return |
| 11 | Capital Market Line (CML) | $E(R_c) = R_f + \dfrac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}\cdot \sigma_c$ | Slope = market price of risk |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance

- **Asset Management:** Used by BlackRock, Vanguard, and Indian firms like HDFC AMC to construct equity-debt hybrid funds (e.g., balanced advantage funds).
- **Robo-Advisors:** Wealth-tech platforms (e.g., INDmoney, Kuvera in India) implement MPT-based dynamic asset allocation.
- **Banking Treasury:** RBI-licensed banks use mean-variance optimization to manage SLR/CRR portfolios.
- **Insurance ALM:** Life insurers (LIC, ICICI Pru) apply MPT to match long-dated liabilities with diversified bond-equity mixes.
- **Project Portfolio Selection:** Kerala State Electricity Board and similar PSUs apply MPT logic to choose generation technology mixes (Solar + Wind + Thermal).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Expected Return of an $N$-Asset Portfolio

**Starting Premise:** Let there be $N$ assets with random returns $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N$. The portfolio's return $R_p$ is a *linear combination* of these:

$$R_p = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i R_i, \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i = 1$$

**Step 1 — Apply the expectation operator** (linearity of expectation allows entry-by-entry operation):

$$E(R_p) = E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i R_i\right]$$

**Step 2 — Distribute the operator** (since $w_i$ are constants, not random variables):

$$E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot E(R_i)$$

**Step 3 — State the result in vector form** (compact for KTU board presentation):

$$E(R_p) = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\mu}$$

where $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)^T$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (E(R_1), E(R_2), \dots, E(R_N))^T$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Variance of a 2-Asset Portfolio (The KTU Showpiece Derivation)

**Starting Premise:** $R_p = w_1 R_1 + w_2 R_2$ with $w_1 + w_2 = 1$.

**Step 1 — Write the deviation from the mean** (portfolio alpha = weighted alpha):

$$R_p - E(R_p) = w_1 [R_1 - E(R_1)] + w_2 [R_2 - E(R_2)]$$

**Step 2 — Square both sides** to obtain the squared deviation:

$$[R_p - E(R_p)]^2 = \{w_1 [R_1 - E(R_1)] + w_2 [R_2 - E(R_2)]\}^2$$

**Step 3 — Expand the square** (binomial expansion with four terms):

$$[R_p - E(R_p)]^2 = w_1^2 [R_1 - E(R_1)]^2 + w_2^2 [R_2 - E(R_2)]^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 [R_1 - E(R_1)][R_2 - E(R_2)]$$

**Step 4 — Take expectations of both sides** (definition of variance on LHS, individual variances and covariance on RHS):

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \, \sigma_{12}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the identity** $\sigma_{12} = \rho_{12}\, \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ to obtain the most exam-friendly form:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho_{12}\, \sigma_1 \sigma_2$$

**Step 6 — Compute standard deviation** (take the positive square root):

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho_{12}\, \sigma_1 \sigma_2}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Global Minimum Variance Portfolio (GMVP) Weights

**Goal:** Minimize $\sigma_p^2(w_1, w_2) = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + (1-w_1)^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 (1-w_1) \sigma_{12}$ subject to $w_1 + w_2 = 1$.

**Step 1 — Substitute $w_2 = 1 - w_1$** to reduce to a single-variable problem:

$$\sigma_p^2(w_1) = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + (1 - w_1)^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 (1 - w_1) \sigma_{12}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $w_1$** (first-order optimality condition):

$$\frac{d\sigma_p^2}{dw_1} = 2 w_1 \sigma_1^2 - 2(1 - w_1) \sigma_2^2 + 2(1 - 2w_1)\sigma_{12} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Simplify and collect the $w_1$ terms:**

$$2 w_1 \sigma_1^2 - 2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 \sigma_2^2 + 2 \sigma_{12} - 4 w_1 \sigma_{12} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Group $w_1$ on the left and constants on the right:**

$$w_1 (\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\sigma_{12}) = \sigma_2^2 - \sigma_{12}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $w_1$:**

$$w_1^{\mathrm{GMV}} = \frac{\sigma_2^2 - \sigma_{12}}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\sigma_{12}}$$

and consequently $w_2^{\mathrm{GMV}} = 1 - w_1^{\mathrm{GMV}}$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem Setup:** Consider two stocks with the following characteristics:
> - Stock A: $E(R_A) = 12\%$, $\sigma_A = 20\%$
> - Stock B: $E(R_B) = 8\%$, $\sigma_B = 15\%$
> - Correlation: $\rho_{AB} = 0.3$
> - Portfolio weights: $w_A = 0.6$, $w_B = 0.4$
> - Risk-free rate: $R_f = 5\%$

**Step 1 — Compute portfolio expected return:**

$$E(R_p) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B) = (0.6)(12) + (0.4)(8) = 7.2 + 3.2 = 10.4\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute covariance:**

$$\sigma_{AB} = \rho_{AB} \cdot \sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B = (0.3)(20)(15) = 90 \quad (\text{in } \%^2 \text{ units})$$

**Step 3 — Compute portfolio variance:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.6)^2 (20)^2 + (0.4)^2 (15)^2 + 2(0.6)(0.4)(90)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each term separately:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.36)(400) + (0.16)(225) + (0.48)(90)$$

**Step 5 — Multiply out:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = 144 + 36 + 43.2 = 223.2 \quad (\%^2)$$

**Step 6 — Take the square root for portfolio standard deviation:**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{223.2} \approx 14.94\%$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Sharpe Ratio:**

$$S_p = \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p} = \frac{10.4 - 5}{14.94} = \frac{5.4}{14.94} \approx 0.3614$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation:** A Sharpe Ratio of **0.3614** indicates the portfolio earns **0.3614 units of excess return per unit of total risk**. A higher Sharpe Ratio is preferred.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Correlation Effect on Portfolio Risk (Numerical Evidence)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a KTU-favorite: "How does correlation affect portfolio variance?"**

| $w_A$ | $w_B$ | $\rho_{AB}$ | $\sigma_p$ (computed) | Diversification Benefit |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1.0 | 0.0 | — | 20.00% | None (single asset) |
| 0.6 | 0.4 | $+1.0$ | 18.00% | **Zero** (perfectly correlated) |
| 0.6 | 0.4 | $+0.3$ | 14.94% | Moderate |
| 0.6 | 0.4 | $0.0$ | 13.40% | Strong |
| 0.6 | 0.4 | $-0.3$ | 11.58% | Very strong |
| 0.6 | 0.4 | $-1.0$ | 9.00% | **Maximum possible** |

**Key Insight:** As $\rho \to -1$, portfolio risk can fall **below** the lowest individual asset's risk — the theoretical maximum of the diversification effect.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MPT Conceptual Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Layer: Asset Universe N Assets] --> B[Parameter Estimation: mu sigma rho]
    B --> C[Compute E of R_p = w transpose mu]
    B --> D[Compute Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    C --> E[Mean Variance Optimizer]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Efficient Frontier Curve]
    E --> G[GMVP: Global Minimum Variance Portfolio]
    E --> H[Tangent Portfolio: Max Sharpe Ratio]
    F --> I[Capital Market Line CML]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Recommendation to Investor]
    G --> J
```

### 4.2 Efficient Frontier Schematic (Curve + Key Portfolios)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EF["Sigma p Risk Axis horizontal"]
        A1[Portfolio with min return high risk]
        A2[Sub Optimal Region Below Curve]
        A3[Unattainable Region Above Curve]
    end
    subgraph FE["E of R_p Return Axis vertical"]
        B1[Efficient Frontier Curve]
        B2[Global Min Variance Portfolio GMVP]
        B3[Tangent Portfolio T Max Sharpe]
        B4[Market Portfolio M when rf available]
    end
    B2 --> B1
    B3 --> B1
    B4 --> B1
    B1 --> A1
    A2 -. .-> B1
    A3 -. .-> B1
```

### 4.3 Diversification Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Risky Asset 1: E R1 sigma 1] --> P[Portfolio w1 R1 + w2 R2 + ... + wN RN]
    R2[Risky Asset 2: E R2 sigma 2] --> P
    R3[Risky Asset N: E RN sigma N] --> P
    R1 --> C[Pairwise Covariances sigma_ij]
    R2 --> C
    R3 --> C
    C --> P
    P --> O1{Idiosyncratic Risk Eliminated as N grows}
    P --> O2{Systematic Risk Persists driven by market correlation}
    O1 --> F[Lower Total Risk sigma p]
    O2 --> F
    F --> EF[Point on Efficient Frontier]
```

### 4.4 Capital Market Line (CML) — Risk-Return Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RL["Return Axis E of R_p"]
        A[Risk Free Asset Rf on Y axis]
        B[Market Portfolio M at tangent]
        C[Efficient Frontier Curve]
        D[CML Slope equals Sharpe of M]
        E[Borrowing Region above M]
        F[Lending Region between Rf and M]
    end
    A --> D
    B --> D
    C -. .-> B
    D --> E
    D --> F
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: MPT Workflow in Practice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Define Asset Universe] --> S2[Step 2: Collect Historical Data]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Estimate mu sigma and Correlation Matrix]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Generate Weight Combinations]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute E of R_p and sigma p for each combo]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Plot all Portfolios in Mean Std Dev space]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Extract Efficient Frontier Upper Lobe]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Select Investor Specific Portfolio via Indifference Curves]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT). State any two assumptions of MPT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Modern Portfolio Theory, introduced by **Harry Markowitz in 1952**, is a normative investment framework that constructs optimal portfolios by simultaneously considering the **expected return** and **risk (variance)** of assets, demonstrating that the risk of an individual asset is less relevant than its **contribution to overall portfolio risk** through covariance with other assets.
- **Any two assumptions (1 mark):** (i) Investors are rational and risk-averse, (ii) Returns are normally distributed, (iii) Single-period investment horizon, (iv) Markets are frictionless.

> **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **What is the Efficient Frontier? Why is it represented by an upward-sloping curve?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The Efficient Frontier is the locus of portfolios in the $(\sigma_p, E(R_p))$ space that offer the **maximum expected return for a given level of risk**, or equivalently, the **minimum risk for a given expected return**. It is the upper boundary of the feasible set of portfolios.
- **Why upward sloping (1 mark):** Higher expected return requires bearing higher risk — consistent with the **risk-return trade-off** principle. No rational investor would accept *more* risk for *less* return, hence the curve must be non-decreasing.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

> **Q. (a)** Two assets, X and Y, have the following characteristics:
> - Asset X: $E(R_X) = 18\%$, $\sigma_X = 25\%$
> - Asset Y: $E(R_Y) = 10\%$, $\sigma_Y = 12\%$
> - Correlation: $\rho_{XY} = 0.25$
>
> Construct a portfolio with $w_X = 0.55$ and $w_Y = 0.45$. Calculate:
> (i) Portfolio expected return
> (ii) Portfolio standard deviation
> (iii) Comment on whether diversification benefit exists. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Covariance computation:**

$$\sigma_{XY} = \rho_{XY} \cdot \sigma_X \cdot \sigma_Y = (0.25)(25)(12) = 75 \quad (\%^2)$$

**Step 2 — Portfolio expected return:**

$$E(R_p) = w_X E(R_X) + w_Y E(R_Y) = (0.55)(18) + (0.45)(10) = 9.9 + 4.5 = 14.4\%$$

**Step 3 — Portfolio variance:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.55)^2 (25)^2 + (0.45)^2 (12)^2 + 2(0.55)(0.45)(75)$$

**Step 4 — Term-by-term evaluation:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.3025)(625) + (0.2025)(144) + (0.495)(75)$$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = 189.0625 + 29.16 + 37.125 = 255.3475$$

**Step 6 — Portfolio standard deviation:**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{255.3475} \approx 15.98\%$$

**Step 7 — Diversification commentary:**
The weighted average of individual standard deviations is $0.55(25) + 0.45(12) = 19.15\%$. Since the portfolio's actual $\sigma_p \approx 15.98\%$ is **lower** than this weighted average, **diversification benefit exists**. This arises because the assets are *imperfectly* correlated ($\rho = 0.25 < 1$).

> **Q. (b)** With reference to the above data, calculate the **Global Minimum Variance Portfolio (GMVP)** weights and its corresponding risk level. Discuss the practical significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — GMVP weight for X:**

$$w_X^{\mathrm{GMV}} = \frac{\sigma_Y^2 - \sigma_{XY}}{\sigma_X^2 + \sigma_Y^2 - 2\sigma_{XY}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$w_X^{\mathrm{GMV}} = \frac{(12)^2 - 75}{(25)^2 + (12)^2 - 2(75)} = \frac{144 - 75}{625 + 144 - 150} = \frac{69}{619}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical value:**

$$w_X^{\mathrm{GMV}} = 0.1115 \quad \text{and} \quad w_Y^{\mathrm{GMV}} = 0.8885$$

**Step 4 — GMVP expected return:**

$$E(R_{\mathrm{GMV}}) = (0.1115)(18) + (0.8885)(10) = 2.007 + 8.885 = 10.892\%$$

**Step 5 — GMVP variance:**

$$\sigma_{\mathrm{GMV}}^2 = (0.1115)^2(625) + (0.8885)^2(144) + 2(0.1115)(0.8885)(75)$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate term by term:**

$$\sigma_{\mathrm{GMV}}^2 = 7.771 + 113.69 + 14.852 = 136.31$$

**Step 7 — GMVP standard deviation:**

$$\sigma_{\mathrm{GMV}} = \sqrt{136.31} \approx 11.68\%$$

**Step 8 — Practical Significance:**
- The GMVP is the *lowest-risk* achievable portfolio within the two-asset universe.
- Notice $w_X = 11.15\%$ — heavily tilted to Y, the *lower-variance* asset.
- It represents the *starting point* of the efficient frontier (the leftmost point of the curve).
- Practically, it is the *anchor portfolio* for ultra-conservative investors (e.g., pension funds, retirement corpus managers).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

> **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the construction of the **Efficient Frontier** in the mean-standard deviation plane. How does the introduction of a **risk-free asset** modify the investment opportunity set? Define the **Capital Market Line (CML)** with its equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction:**
- Step 1A: Enumerate all $N$ assets and estimate $(\mu_i, \sigma_i, \rho_{ij})$ from historical data.
- Step 1B: For each weight vector $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, \dots, w_N)$ satisfying $\sum w_i = 1$, compute the pair $(E(R_p), \sigma_p)$.
- Step 1C: Plot all feasible portfolios in the $(\sigma_p, E(R_p))$ plane.
- Step 1D: The boundary of the resulting cloud that is *non-dominated* (i.e., not lying strictly below/left of another feasible portfolio) forms the **Efficient Frontier**.
- Step 1E: It is *hyperbola-shaped* in the variance-return plane and a *concave curve* in the standard deviation-return plane.

**Step 2 — Introduction of Risk-Free Asset:**
- When a risk-free asset $R_f$ is added, the *entire efficient frontier* becomes **inaccessible** except for one special portfolio called the **Tangent Portfolio (T)**.
- T is the unique risky portfolio on the frontier that, when combined with $R_f$, offers the **highest attainable Sharpe Ratio**.
- The new investment opportunity set is the straight line from $(0, R_f)$ to T, extended indefinitely (allowable leverage).

**Step 3 — Capital Market Line (CML):**

$$E(R_c) = R_f + \left[\frac{E(R_T) - R_f}{\sigma_T}\right] \sigma_c$$

- The **slope** is the **Sharpe Ratio** of the tangent portfolio.
- **CML** represents all efficient combinations of $R_f$ and the Tangent Portfolio.
- Portfolios to the **left** of T → *lending* (invest partly in $R_f$).
- Portfolios to the **right** of T → *borrowing at $R_f$* (leverage).

> **Q. (b)** Consider three assets P, Q, R with the following expected returns and standard deviations: $E(R_P)=15\%,\ \sigma_P=22\%$; $E(R_Q)=10\%,\ \sigma_Q=14\%$; $E(R_R)=18\%,\ \sigma_R=30\%$. The correlation matrix is:
>
> $\rho_{PQ} = 0.4$, $\rho_{PR} = 0.6$, $\rho_{QR} = 0.3$.
>
> An investor allocates $w_P = 0.5$, $w_Q = 0.3$, $w_R = 0.2$. Compute the portfolio's expected return, variance, standard deviation, and Sharpe Ratio (assume $R_f = 6\%$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Covariance matrix computation:**

$$\sigma_{PQ} = (0.4)(22)(14) = 123.2, \quad \sigma_{PR} = (0.6)(22)(30) = 396, \quad \sigma_{QR} = (0.3)(14)(30) = 126$$

**Step 2 — Portfolio expected return:**

$$E(R_p) = (0.5)(15) + (0.3)(10) + (0.2)(18) = 7.5 + 3.0 + 3.6 = 14.1\%$$

**Step 3 — Portfolio variance** (using $\sigma_p^2 = \sum_i \sum_j w_i w_j \sigma_{ij}$):

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_P^2 \sigma_P^2 + w_Q^2 \sigma_Q^2 + w_R^2 \sigma_R^2 + 2 w_P w_Q \sigma_{PQ} + 2 w_P w_R \sigma_{PR} + 2 w_Q w_R \sigma_{QR}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.25)(484) + (0.09)(196) + (0.04)(900) + 2(0.5)(0.3)(123.2) + 2(0.5)(0.2)(396) + 2(0.3)(0.2)(126)$$

**Step 5 — Multiply each term:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = 121 + 17.64 + 36 + 36.96 + 79.2 + 15.12$$

**Step 6 — Sum:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = 305.92 \quad (\%^2)$$

**Step 7 — Standard deviation:**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{305.92} \approx 17.49\%$$

**Step 8 — Sharpe Ratio:**

$$S_p = \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p} = \frac{14.1 - 6}{17.49} = \frac{8.1}{17.49} \approx 0.4631$$

**Step 9 — Interpretation:** A Sharpe Ratio of **0.4631** indicates **moderate risk-adjusted performance**. The portfolio yields **0.4631% excess return per 1% of risk**, surpassing the typical equity benchmark of ~0.35 in Indian markets.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones (KTU Board Examiner Observations):**
>
> 1. **Covariance ≠ Correlation:** Students frequently substitute $\sigma_{12}$ in place of $\rho_{12}$ or vice-versa. **Always check** whether the problem gives *correlation* (use $\rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2$) or *covariance directly*.
>
> 2. **Weight Constraint Ignored:** Many answers forget that $\sum w_i = 1$. If the problem specifies *three* assets with only *two* weights given, compute the third as $w_3 = 1 - w_1 - w_2$.
>
> 3. **Variance vs. Standard Deviation:** Final answers must clearly distinguish between $\sigma_p^2$ (variance, in $\%^2$) and $\sigma_p$ (standard deviation, in \%). Examiners **deduct 0.5 mark** if these are interchanged.
>
> 4. **No Square Root of Negative:** Diversification benefit arises only when $\rho < 1$. If asked to "show diversification," students must explicitly state the correlation condition.
>
> 5. **Capital Market Line vs. Security Market Line:** CML applies *only* to efficient portfolios (a subset); SML applies to *all* assets (using $\beta$ instead of $\sigma$). Confusing them costs **2 marks** in 14-mark questions.
>
> 6. **Units in Final Answer:** Always express returns and risks in **percentage** terms consistently. Mixing decimals (0.144) and percentages (14.4%) is a common careless error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize This Block**

- [x] **MPT Author:** Harry M. Markowitz, 1952, **Nobel 1990**.
- [x] **Two-Asset Portfolio Variance:** $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$. **This is the single most important formula.**
- [x] **Covariance Identity:** $\sigma_{ij} = \rho_{ij} \sigma_i \sigma_j$; range of $\rho_{ij} \in [-1, +1]$.
- [x] **Efficient Frontier:** Upward-sloping, concave curve in $(\sigma_p, E(R_p))$ space; upper boundary of the feasible set.
- [x] **GMVP (Global Minimum Variance Portfolio):** Weights via $w_1 = (\sigma_2^2 - \sigma_{12}) / (\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\sigma_{12})$; the **leftmost** point of the frontier.
- [x] **Tangent Portfolio:** Touches the efficient frontier at the point of maximum slope from the $R_f$ point on the y-axis.
- [x] **CML Equation:** $E(R_c) = R_f + [(E(R_T) - R_f)/\sigma_T] \sigma_c$; slope = Sharpe Ratio of T.
- [x] **Sharpe Ratio:** $S_p = [E(R_p) - R_f] / \sigma_p$; higher is better.
- [x] **Diversification Logic:** $\sigma_p^2 \to \bar{\rho} \bar{\sigma}^2$ as $N \to \infty$; idiosyncratic risk vanishes, systematic risk persists.
- [x] **Six Core Assumptions:** Rational risk-averse investors, single-period horizon, normal returns, homogeneous expectations, frictionless markets, divisible assets.
- [x] **No short-selling (long-only constraint):** $w_i \geq 0$ — *extension* not always assumed; clarify in exam.
- [x] **Limitation of MPT:** Assumes normality (fat tails ignored), uses historical estimates (look-ahead bias), ignores transaction costs and taxes.
- [x] **Relationship to CAPM:** MPT is the *static* foundation; CAPM is the *equilibrium* extension that prices individual assets using $\beta$ instead of $\sigma$.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Modern Portfolio Theory & Efficient Frontier.**
> These notes are optimized for **KTU 2024 Scheme PECDT742** Board Examinations. For numerical practice, redo the worked examples in SECTION 3.4 and SECTION 5.2 (both Q-A and Q-B) without referring to the solutions.
