topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT742 - FINANCIAL DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimization Algorithms - Gradient Descent, Genetic Algorithms for Portfolio Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Optimization in Finance — The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimization** is the mathematical process of selecting the best element (with regard to some criterion) from a set of available alternatives. In **Financial Data Science**, optimization underpins every quantitative decision: choosing asset weights, calibrating risk models, fitting option pricing surfaces, and minimizing tracking error.

In the context of this module, we study two of the most important classes of optimization algorithms used to solve the **Portfolio Optimization Problem**:

1. **Gradient Descent (GD)** — a *first-order, calculus-based, deterministic* iterative method.
2. **Genetic Algorithms (GA)** — a *population-based, stochastic, metaheuristic* search method inspired by Darwinian evolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Portfolio Optimization** refers to the formal allocation of capital across a basket of financial assets so that the resulting portfolio maximizes expected return for a given level of risk (or equivalently, minimizes risk for a given expected return). This is rooted in **Harry Markowitz's Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)**, published in **1952**, for which he later received the **Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences (1990)**.

---

## 1.2 Gradient Descent — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Gradient Descent** is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the local minimum of a differentiable function $f(\theta)$. At each iteration, the parameters $\theta$ are updated by taking a step proportional to the *negative* of the gradient of the objective function evaluated at the current point.

The update rule is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \alpha \cdot \nabla f(\theta_{t})$$

where $\alpha > 0$ is the **learning rate** (also called the *step size*), and $\nabla f(\theta_{t})$ is the gradient vector of $f$ with respect to $\theta$ at iteration $t$.

### 🔭 Intuitive Analogy — The Blindfolded Mountain Walker

Imagine you are standing on a mountain in thick fog, and you need to reach the valley floor (the lowest point). You cannot see the terrain, but you can **feel the slope** beneath your feet. The direction of steepest descent tells you which way is downhill.

- You take a **step** in the steepest downhill direction.
- The **size of your step** is the learning rate $\alpha$ — too small, and you take forever; too large, and you overshoot the valley and may even climb the opposite slope.
- You keep sensing the slope and stepping until the ground feels flat (gradient $\approx 0$).

> [!TIP]
> **For financial students**: The "mountain" is the **loss surface** of a portfolio's risk-return profile. The "valley" is the optimal allocation that maximizes the **Sharpe Ratio** or minimizes the portfolio's **variance**.

---

## 1.3 Genetic Algorithms — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a metaheuristic inspired by the process of **natural selection** that belongs to the larger class of **Evolutionary Algorithms (EA)**. GA uses a population of candidate solutions (called *chromosomes* or *individuals*), encoded as strings of symbols (typically binary or real-valued), and iteratively applies biologically inspired operators — **selection, crossover, and mutation** — to evolve better solutions over generations.

### 🔭 Intuitive Analogy — The Survival-of-the-Fittest Portfolio Manager

Imagine you have **1,000 junior portfolio managers**, each proposing a different asset allocation (e.g., "30% stocks, 50% bonds, 20% gold"). At the end of each quarter:

- You **measure the fitness** of each manager's portfolio (e.g., Sharpe Ratio).
- The **best-performing managers** are promoted and given more "copies" of their allocation strategies.
- Pairs of successful managers **combine** their strategies (crossover) — like blending two recipes.
- Occasionally, a random **mutation** introduces a small surprise (e.g., trying a 1% allocation to bitcoin) to explore new ideas.

Over many generations, the population of allocations **evolves** toward high-Sharpe-Ratio portfolios — without needing calculus, derivatives, or gradient information. This is precisely why GA excels in **non-convex, noisy, discontinuous** portfolio landscapes where gradient methods fail.

---

## 1.4 Why Two Different Algorithms? — A Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gradient Descent** and **Genetic Algorithms** are not competitors — they are **complementary tools** for different problem regimes.

| Property | Gradient Descent | Genetic Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Information used | Requires **gradient** $\nabla f$ | Requires only **fitness function** value |
| Solution type | **Single** point per iteration | **Population** of points per iteration |
| Optimality | Converges to **local** optimum | Approximates **global** optimum |
| Objective landscape | Smooth, differentiable | Can handle **non-differentiable**, discontinuous |
| Computational cost per iteration | **Cheap** (one gradient eval) | **Expensive** (evaluate entire population) |
| Determinism | **Deterministic** (given $\alpha$) | **Stochastic** (random operators) |
| Best suited for | Convex problems, neural net training | Non-convex, multi-modal, combinatorial |

The constants we will repeatedly use throughout this module:

- **Risk-free rate** $r_f$ — typically the **10-year Treasury yield**.
- **Learning rate** $\alpha$ — usually in the range $\mathbf{10^{-4}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-1}}$.
- **Population size** $N_{pop}$ — typically **50 to 200** for portfolio problems.
- **Mutation rate** $p_m$ — typically **0.001 to 0.05**.
- **Crossover rate** $p_c$ — typically **0.6 to 0.95**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Gradient Descent — Operational Mechanics

Gradient Descent operates on the following structured logic:

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Initialize** parameters $\theta_0$ (randomly or using domain knowledge).
2. **Compute the gradient** $\nabla f(\theta_t)$ at the current point. The gradient is the vector of partial derivatives:
   
   $$\nabla f(\theta) = \left[ \frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta_1}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta_2}, \ldots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta_n} \right]^T$$

3. **Update the parameters** by stepping opposite to the gradient direction:
   
   $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \alpha \cdot \nabla f(\theta_{t})$$

4. **Check convergence** using a stopping criterion such as:
   - Norm of the gradient falls below a tolerance: $\Vert \nabla f(\theta_t) \Vert < \epsilon$
   - Change in objective value is negligible: $\vert f(\theta_{t+1}) - f(\theta_t) \vert < \epsilon$
   - Maximum number of iterations reached: $t = T_{max}$

5. **Return** the final parameters $\theta^*$ as the approximate optimum.

### Variants of Gradient Descent (KTU-Favored Knowledge)

| Variant | Update Rule | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$ | Uses entire dataset per update; stable but slow on big data |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | Uses **one sample** per update; noisy but fast |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t; \mathcal{B}_t)$ | Uses a batch of $B$ samples; best compromise |
| **Momentum-based GD** | $v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$, then $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_{t+1}$ | Accelerates convergence by accumulating velocity |
| **Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation)** | Combines momentum + RMSProp with bias correction | Industry-standard for deep learning |

---

## 2.2 Gradient Descent Applied to Mean-Variance Portfolio Optimization

The **Markowitz Mean-Variance Optimization (MVO)** problem is the canonical portfolio optimization formulation:

$$\min_{w} \quad \frac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w$$

subject to the constraints:

$$w^T \mu = \mu^* \quad \text{(target return constraint)}$$

$$w^T \mathbf{1} = 1 \quad \text{(fully invested budget)}$$

$$w_i \geq 0 \quad \text{(no short-selling, optional)}$$

where:

- $w \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the vector of portfolio weights.
- $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **covariance matrix** of asset returns.
- $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the vector of expected asset returns.
- $\mu^*$ is the **target expected portfolio return**.
- $\mathbf{1}$ is a vector of ones.

### The Lagrangian Formulation

By introducing Lagrange multipliers $\lambda$ (for return) and $\gamma$ (for budget), the Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda, \gamma) = \frac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w - \lambda(w^T \mu - \mu^*) - \gamma(w^T \mathbf{1} - 1)$$

Setting the gradient with respect to $w$ to zero yields the **first-order condition (KKT)**:

$$\Sigma w - \lambda \mu - \gamma \mathbf{1} = 0$$

Solving for $w$:

$$w^* = \lambda \Sigma^{-1} \mu + \gamma \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}$$

This is a **convex quadratic program (QP)** with a closed-form solution. Gradient Descent can solve this iteratively by minimizing:

$$f(w) = \frac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w - \lambda (w^T \mu - \mu^*) - \gamma (w^T \mathbf{1} - 1)$$

with $\nabla f(w) = \Sigma w - \lambda \mu - \gamma \mathbf{1}$.

---

## 2.3 Genetic Algorithms — Operational Mechanics

GA works on a **population** of candidate solutions and evolves them through generations. The detailed step-by-step operational logic follows.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Initialization** — Randomly generate an initial population of $N$ candidate solutions (chromosomes). For portfolio optimization, each chromosome is a vector of asset weights $w = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$ subject to the budget constraint $\sum w_i = 1$.

2. **Fitness Evaluation** — For each chromosome, compute a fitness function. Common choices for portfolios include:
   - **Sharpe Ratio**: $SR(w) = \dfrac{w^T \mu - r_f}{\sqrt{w^T \Sigma w}}$
   - **Negative portfolio variance**: $-\sigma_p^2 = -w^T \Sigma w$
   - **Risk-adjusted return**: $w^T \mu - \lambda \cdot w^T \Sigma w$

3. **Selection** — Choose parent chromosomes for reproduction based on fitness. Common methods:
   - **Roulette Wheel Selection**: Probability $\propto$ fitness.
   - **Tournament Selection**: Pick $k$ individuals, select the best.
   - **Rank-Based Selection**: Probability based on rank, not raw fitness.

4. **Crossover (Recombination)** — Combine pairs of parents to produce offspring. Methods include:
   - **Single-Point Crossover**: Cut at one position and swap tails.
   - **Two-Point Crossover**: Cut at two positions and swap the middle.
   - **Uniform Crossover**: Each gene independently chosen from either parent.
   - **Arithmetic Crossover** (for real-valued portfolios): $w_{child} = \beta w_{parent1} + (1 - \beta) w_{parent2}$.

5. **Mutation** — Introduce small random perturbations to maintain diversity:
   - **Gaussian Mutation**: $w_i \leftarrow w_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_m^2)$
   - **Uniform Mutation**: $w_i \leftarrow w_i + U(-\delta, \delta)$
   - **Bit-Flip Mutation**: For binary encodings, flip bits with probability $p_m$.

6. **Replacement** — Form the new generation, possibly using **elitism** (carry over the best $e$ individuals unchanged).

7. **Termination** — Stop when:
   - Maximum generations reached.
   - Fitness improvement falls below threshold.
   - Population convergence detected.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Quick-Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you must know for this topic. Master these for the 14-mark derivations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GD Update Rule | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla f(\theta_t)$ | $\alpha$ is the learning rate |
| 2 | GD Convergence Condition | $\vert \nabla f(\theta^*) \vert = 0$ | At optimum, gradient vanishes |
| 3 | Optimal Step Size (Quadratic) | $\alpha^* = \dfrac{(g^T g)}{(g^T H g)}$ | $H$ is Hessian |
| 4 | Portfolio Expected Return | $E[R_p] = w^T \mu$ | Linear in weights |
| 5 | Portfolio Variance | $\sigma_p^2 = w^T \Sigma w$ | Quadratic in weights |
| 6 | Portfolio Standard Deviation | $\sigma_p = \sqrt{w^T \Sigma w}$ | Square root of variance |
| 7 | Sharpe Ratio | $SR = \dfrac{w^T \mu - r_f}{\sqrt{w^T \Sigma w}}$ | Universal performance metric |
| 8 | MVO Objective (Variance) | $\min_w \tfrac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w$ | Markowitz (1952) |
| 9 | MVO Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w - \lambda(w^T \mu - \mu^*) - \gamma(w^T \mathbf{1} - 1)$ | Two constraints |
| 10 | Closed-Form MVO Solution | $w^* = \lambda \Sigma^{-1} \mu + \gamma \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}$ | Analytical optimum |
| 11 | GD on MVO Objective | $\nabla_w f(w) = \Sigma w - \lambda \mu - \gamma \mathbf{1}$ | Plug into GD rule |
| 12 | Lagrangian Multipliers | $\lambda = \dfrac{C \mu^* - A}{D}$, $\gamma = \dfrac{B - A \mu^*}{D}$ | From $\Sigma$ decomposition |
| 13 | GA Fitness (Sharpe) | $F(w) = \dfrac{w^T \mu - r_f}{\sqrt{w^T \Sigma w}}$ | Real-valued encoding |
| 14 | Roulette Wheel Probability | $P_i = \dfrac{F_i}{\sum_j F_j}$ | For $i$-th individual |
| 15 | Arithmetic Crossover | $w_{child} = \beta w_{p1} + (1 - \beta) w_{p2}$ | $\beta \in [0, 1]$ |
| 16 | Gaussian Mutation | $w_i \leftarrow w_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_m^2)$ | $\sigma_m$ is mutation std. |
| 17 | Capital Market Line (CML) | $E[R_p] = r_f + \dfrac{E[R_m] - r_f}{\sigma_m} \cdot \sigma_p$ | Tangent portfolio |
| 18 | Efficient Frontier | $\mu_p^{\min}(\sigma_p) = $ lower envelope of $(\sigma_p, \mu_p)$ points | Set of optimal portfolios |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility**: GD is the workhorse of every machine learning pipeline used in finance — from training **LSTM stock price predictors** to calibrating **stochastic volatility models** to fitting **yield curves**. GA is widely used in **index tracking**, **optimal execution**, and **feature selection** where the loss landscape is rugged and non-differentiable.

---

## 2.5 Why Gradient Descent Can Fail in Portfolio Optimization

> [!WARNING]
> Despite its elegance, GD can fail catastrophically in portfolio problems when:
> - $\Sigma$ is **ill-conditioned** (near-singular covariance matrix) → divergence.
> - Constraints are **non-convex** (e.g., cardinality constraints: "pick exactly 10 assets") → GD cannot enforce discrete choices.
> - The objective contains **discrete terms** (transaction costs with fixed components) → non-differentiable.

This is precisely why **GA is favored** in practice for **discrete, cardinality-constrained, multi-objective** portfolio problems.

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rule from First Principles

We derive the update rule using the **first-order Taylor expansion** of $f(\theta)$ around $\theta_t$:

$$f(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) \approx f(\theta_t) + \nabla f(\theta_t)^T \Delta\theta$$

Our goal is to find the perturbation $\Delta\theta$ that **minimizes** the linearized function. This is a simple linear program in $\Delta\theta$:

$$\min_{\Delta\theta} \quad \nabla f(\theta_t)^T \Delta\theta \quad \text{subject to} \quad \Vert \Delta\theta \Vert \leq \alpha$$

By the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality**, $\nabla f(\theta_t)^T \Delta\theta \geq -\Vert \nabla f(\theta_t) \Vert \cdot \Vert \Delta\theta \Vert$, with equality when $\Delta\theta$ points opposite to $\nabla f(\theta_t)$. The minimizing perturbation is:

$$\Delta\theta^* = -\alpha \cdot \frac{\nabla f(\theta_t)}{\Vert \nabla f(\theta_t) \Vert}$$

Substituting the perturbation $\Delta\theta$ as $-\alpha \nabla f(\theta_t)$ (absorbing the norm into $\alpha$) yields the canonical update:

$$\boxed{\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \alpha \cdot \nabla f(\theta_{t})}$$

---

## 3.2 Complete Derivation of the Closed-Form Mean-Variance Optimum

We derive the Markowitz weights analytically. Start with the unconstrained Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda, \gamma) = \frac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w - \lambda (w^T \mu - \mu^*) - \gamma (w^T \mathbf{1} - 1)$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to $w$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = \Sigma w - \lambda \mu - \gamma \mathbf{1} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $w$:**

Since $\Sigma$ is positive-definite (variance-covariance of linearly independent assets), $\Sigma^{-1}$ exists:

$$w^* = \Sigma^{-1} (\lambda \mu + \gamma \mathbf{1}) = \lambda \Sigma^{-1} \mu + \gamma \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}$$

**Step 3 — Define intermediate scalars** to simplify substitution. Let:

$$A = \mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu, \quad B = \mu^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu, \quad C = \mathbf{1}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}$$

$$D = BC - A^2$$

**Step 4 — Impose the two constraints** by premultiplying the optimal weights by $\mu^T$ and $\mathbf{1}^T$:

$$w^{*T} \mu = \mu^* \implies \lambda B + \gamma A = \mu^*$$

$$w^{*T} \mathbf{1} = 1 \implies \lambda A + \gamma C = 1$$

**Step 5 — Solve the 2×2 linear system** for $\lambda$ and $\gamma$ using Cramer's rule:

$$\lambda = \frac{C \mu^* - A}{D}, \qquad \gamma = \frac{B - A \mu^*}{D}$$

**Step 6 — Final closed-form weights:**

$$\boxed{w^* = \frac{C \mu^* - A}{D} \cdot \Sigma^{-1} \mu + \frac{B - A \mu^*}{D} \cdot \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}}$$

This is the **exact** Markowitz frontier — no iteration required. We can, however, also reach this solution iteratively via Gradient Descent, which is what numerical solvers do in practice for **large-scale problems** (hundreds or thousands of assets).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Gradient Descent on a 2-Asset Portfolio

Consider a 2-asset problem with:

$$\mu = \begin{bmatrix} 0.10 \\ 0.06 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0.01 \\ 0.01 & 0.0225 \end{bmatrix}$$

We minimize portfolio variance with a target return $\mu^* = 0.08$ and full investment constraint. The Lagrangian is:

$$f(w_1, w_2) = \frac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w - \lambda (w^T \mu - 0.08) - \gamma (w_1 + w_2 - 1)$$

The gradient is:

$$\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} \Sigma_{11} w_1 + \Sigma_{12} w_2 - \lambda \mu_1 - \gamma \\ \Sigma_{21} w_1 + \Sigma_{22} w_2 - \lambda \mu_2 - \gamma \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 w_1 + 0.01 w_2 - 0.10 \lambda - \gamma \\ 0.01 w_1 + 0.0225 w_2 - 0.06 \lambda - \gamma \end{bmatrix}$$

**GD Iteration trace** (taking $\alpha = 0.5$, starting from $w^{(0)} = (0.5, 0.5)$, $\lambda = 0.5$, $\gamma = 0.5$):

- **Iteration 0:** $w = (0.5000, 0.5000)$, gradient components: $-0.0800, -0.0388$. Variance $= 0.5 \cdot w^T \Sigma w = 0.01988$.
- **Iteration 1:** Update $w_1 \leftarrow 0.5 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.08) = 0.5400$; $w_2 \leftarrow 0.5 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.0388) = 0.5194$. New $w = (0.5400, 0.5194)$. Variance $\approx 0.02027$.
- **Iteration 2:** Gradient at $(0.5400, 0.5194)$: $-0.0796, -0.0387$. Update: $w = (0.5798, 0.5388)$. Variance $\approx 0.02067$.
- After ~**50 iterations**, weights converge to the analytical optimum $w^* \approx (0.6667, 0.3333)$.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Genetic Algorithm on a 3-Asset Portfolio

**Setup**: 3 assets with $\mu = (0.12, 0.08, 0.05)^T$, $\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0.005 & 0.002 \\ 0.005 & 0.0225 & 0.003 \\ 0.002 & 0.003 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix}$, $r_f = 0.02$.

**Step 1 — Initialize population** of $N = 4$ chromosomes (each a valid weight vector summing to 1):

| Chromosome | $w_1$ | $w_2$ | $w_3$ | $\sum w_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | 0.50 | 0.30 | 0.20 | 1.00 |
| $C_2$ | 0.20 | 0.50 | 0.30 | 1.00 |
| $C_3$ | 0.70 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 1.00 |
| $C_4$ | 0.30 | 0.40 | 0.30 | 1.00 |

**Step 2 — Fitness evaluation** (using Sharpe Ratio $SR = \dfrac{w^T \mu - r_f}{\sqrt{w^T \Sigma w}}$):

| Chromosome | $w^T \mu$ | $w^T \Sigma w$ | Sharpe Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | $0.5(0.12)+0.3(0.08)+0.2(0.05)=0.094$ | $0.01840$ | $\dfrac{0.074}{\sqrt{0.01840}} = 0.5454$ |
| $C_2$ | $0.2(0.12)+0.5(0.08)+0.3(0.05)=0.079$ | $0.01243$ | $\dfrac{0.059}{\sqrt{0.01243}} = 0.5292$ |
| $C_3$ | $0.7(0.12)+0.1(0.08)+0.2(0.05)=0.102$ | $0.02254$ | $\dfrac{0.082}{\sqrt{0.02254}} = 0.5463$ |
| $C_4$ | $0.3(0.12)+0.4(0.08)+0.3(0.05)=0.077$ | $0.01357$ | $\dfrac{0.057}{\sqrt{0.01357}} = 0.4893$ |

**Step 3 — Roulette Wheel Selection Probabilities** ($P_i = F_i / \sum F_j$):

$$\sum F_i = 0.5454 + 0.5292 + 0.5463 + 0.4893 = 2.1102$$

$$P_1 = 0.2585, \quad P_2 = 0.2508, \quad P_3 = 0.2589, \quad P_4 = 0.2319$$

**Step 4 — Crossover** (single-point at gene index 1, parents $C_1$ and $C_3$):

- Offspring $O_1$: $(0.50, \mathbf{0.10}, \mathbf{0.20}) = (0.50, 0.10, 0.20)$, but must renormalize: sum $= 0.80$, so divide by 0.80 → $O_1 = (0.625, 0.125, 0.250)$.
- Offspring $O_2$: $(0.20, \mathbf{0.30}, \mathbf{0.20}) = (0.70, 0.30, 0.20)$ already sums to 1.00 — but wait, the source genes here need rechecking: $C_1 = (0.50, 0.30, 0.20)$ and $C_3 = (0.70, 0.10, 0.20)$. With single-point crossover after index 0, child1 takes gene 0 from $C_1$ and genes 1,2 from $C_3$ → $(0.50, 0.10, 0.20)$. Child2 takes gene 0 from $C_3$ and genes 1,2 from $C_1$ → $(0.70, 0.30, 0.20)$.

**Step 5 — Gaussian Mutation** with $\sigma_m = 0.02$ on $O_1$:

$O_1 = (0.625, 0.125, 0.250)$. Adding $\mathcal{N}(0, 0.02^2)$ to the second gene: $0.125 + 0.015 = 0.140$. Re-normalize: sum $= 0.625 + 0.140 + 0.250 = 1.015$, divide → $(0.615, 0.138, 0.246)$.

**Step 6 — Fitness of offspring**:

$O_1$ (mutated): $w^T \mu = 0.615(0.12) + 0.138(0.08) + 0.246(0.05) = 0.0993$; $w^T \Sigma w \approx 0.0195$; $SR \approx \dfrac{0.0793}{0.1396} = 0.5681$ — **better than all parents!**

This single numerical exercise illustrates the **exploration power** of GA: even random mutations can find improved solutions that deterministic gradient methods might miss.

---

## 3.5 Convergence Theorem for Gradient Descent on Convex Functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (GD Convergence on Convex Smooth Functions)**: If $f$ is convex and $L$-smooth (gradient is Lipschitz continuous with constant $L$), then Gradient Descent with step size $\alpha \leq \dfrac{1}{L}$ satisfies:
> 
> $$f(\theta_t) - f(\theta^*) \leq \frac{\Vert \theta_0 - \theta^* \Vert^2}{2 \alpha t}$$
> 
> The convergence rate is $\mathcal{O}(1/t)$ — *sublinear*. For **strongly convex** $f$, the rate improves to **linear convergence**: $f(\theta_{t+1}) - f(\theta^*) \leq c \cdot (f(\theta_t) - f(\theta^*))$ with $0 < c < 1$.

# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Portfolio Optimization Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the end-to-end pipeline that links both algorithms into a unified portfolio optimization workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Historical Asset Returns Data] --> B1[Compute Mean Vector mu]
    A1 --> B2[Compute Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    B1 --> C1[Construct Markowitz Objective]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1{Choose Optimizer}
    D1 -- Smooth, Convex --> E1[Gradient Descent Solver]
    D1 -- Non-Convex, Discrete --> F1[Genetic Algorithm Solver]
    E1 --> G1[Iterative Weight Update]
    F1 --> G2[Population Evolution Loop]
    G1 --> H1[Optimal Weights w star]
    G2 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Compute Portfolio Return and Risk]
    I1 --> J1[Sharpe Ratio and Efficient Frontier]
    J1 --> K1[Investment Decision Output]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation**: The diagram shows that *both* GD and GA converge to the same output block (Optimal Weights). They differ only in the **path taken** — GD uses the gradient, GA uses evolutionary operators. The pipeline is **model-agnostic at the output layer**, which is why portfolio analytics tools (e.g., **QuantConnect, Portfolio Visualizer, Bloomberg PORT**) accept solutions from any optimizer.

---

## 4.2 Gradient Descent Iteration Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Initialize theta zero] --> S1[Compute Gradient of f at theta t]
    S1 --> S2[Multiply Gradient by Learning Rate alpha]
    S2 --> S3[Subtract Step from theta t to get theta t plus 1]
    S3 --> S4{Check Convergence}
    S4 -- No --> S1
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Return Optimal theta star]
    S5 --> S6[End]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical GD loop. The **check convergence** block tests any of the three criteria discussed in Section 2.1: gradient norm, objective change, or iteration limit.

---

## 4.3 Genetic Algorithm Cycle Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G0[Start Initialize Population of N chromosomes] --> G1[Evaluate Fitness of Each Individual]
    G1 --> G2[Selection Roulette or Tournament]
    G2 --> G3[Crossover Combine Parent Chromosomes]
    G3 --> G4[Mutation Add Random Perturbations]
    G4 --> G5[Replacement Form New Generation with Elitism]
    G5 --> G6{Termination Condition Met}
    G6 -- No --> G1
    G6 -- Yes --> G7[Return Best Chromosome as Optimal Solution]
    G7 --> G8[End]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the **Selection → Crossover → Mutation → Replacement** cycle forms a *closed loop*, while the entire process begins with a **population**, not a single point. This is the defining structural difference from GD and is the source of GA's **global search capability**.

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Comparison Diagram — Loss Surface Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Convex Loss Surface Single Basin] --> O1[Gradient Descent finds global minimum reliably]
    L2[Non Convex Loss Surface Multiple Basins] --> O2[Gradient Descent gets trapped in local minimum]
    L2 --> O3[Genetic Algorithm explores multiple basins via population]
    O3 --> O4[Higher chance of finding global optimum]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Insight**: When asked *why* GA outperforms GD on certain portfolio problems, the canonical answer is: **GA maintains population diversity, allowing it to escape local optima where gradient information alone would lead to stagnation**. This diagram crystallizes that intuition.

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the working principle of the Gradient Descent algorithm. State its update rule and discuss the role of the learning rate.**

#### Model Answer (Examiner's Key):

Gradient Descent is an iterative first-order optimization algorithm used to find the minimum of a differentiable function $f(\theta)$. At each iteration $t$, the parameters are updated by stepping in the direction opposite to the gradient:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \alpha \nabla f(\theta_{t})$$

**Working Principle:**

1. **Initialize** the parameter vector $\theta_0$.
2. **Compute the gradient** $\nabla f(\theta_t)$ at the current iterate.
3. **Update** the parameters by moving against the gradient direction scaled by $\alpha$.
4. **Repeat** until $\Vert \nabla f(\theta_t) \Vert < \epsilon$ or maximum iterations reached.

**Role of Learning Rate $\alpha$:**

- **Too small** ($\alpha \to 0$): Slow convergence, may stall before reaching the minimum.
- **Too large** ($\alpha \to \infty$): Overshooting, oscillation, divergence.
- **Optimal** ($\alpha \approx 1/L$ for $L$-smooth $f$): Fast, stable convergence.

The learning rate **controls the step size**, balancing convergence speed against numerical stability. In portfolio optimization, a carefully tuned $\alpha$ ensures weights converge to the Markowitz efficient frontier without violating constraints. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List and briefly explain any three operators used in a Genetic Algorithm. How does GA differ from traditional gradient-based optimization?**

#### Model Answer (Examiner's Key):

Three core GA operators are:

1. **Selection** — Chooses parent chromosomes from the population with probability proportional to their fitness. Methods include *Roulette Wheel*, *Tournament*, and *Rank-Based* selection. Fitter individuals have higher chance of reproduction.

2. **Crossover** — Recombines two parent chromosomes to produce offspring. For real-valued portfolio weights, *Arithmetic Crossover* is used: $w_{child} = \beta w_{parent1} + (1-\beta) w_{parent2}$. This blends portfolio strategies.

3. **Mutation** — Introduces small random perturbations to offspring genes, e.g., $w_i \leftarrow w_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_m^2)$. Mutation preserves genetic diversity and prevents premature convergence.

**Differences from Gradient-Based Methods:**

| Aspect | Genetic Algorithm | Gradient Descent |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient Required? | No | Yes |
| Population vs. Point | Population-based | Single point |
| Optimality | Global (approximate) | Local (exact for convex) |
| Objective Landscape | Any (non-differentiable OK) | Differentiable only |

GA's ability to operate **without gradient information** makes it suitable for problems with discrete variables, transaction costs, and cardinality constraints — common in real portfolio construction. **(3 marks)**

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the gradient descent update rule for minimizing a differentiable function $f(\theta)$ using the first-order Taylor approximation. Discuss the conditions for convergence. (7 marks)**

**(b) Apply the Gradient Descent algorithm to the mean-variance portfolio optimization problem. Formulate the objective, derive the gradient, and write down the iterative update equation. (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

### Part (a) — Derivation of GD Update Rule

**Step 1:** Approximate $f$ near $\theta_t$ using the first-order Taylor expansion:

$$f(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) \approx f(\theta_t) + \nabla f(\theta_t)^T \Delta\theta$$

**Step 2:** We want to choose $\Delta\theta$ to minimize the linearized function subject to $\Vert \Delta\theta \Vert \leq \alpha$:

$$\min_{\Delta\theta} \nabla f(\theta_t)^T \Delta\theta$$

**Step 3:** By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:

$$\nabla f(\theta_t)^T \Delta\theta \geq -\Vert \nabla f(\theta_t) \Vert \cdot \Vert \Delta\theta \Vert$$

Equality holds when $\Delta\theta$ points opposite to the gradient.

**Step 4:** The optimal perturbation is:

$$\Delta\theta^* = -\alpha \cdot \frac{\nabla f(\theta_t)}{\Vert \nabla f(\theta_t) \Vert}$$

In practice, we absorb the normalization into $\alpha$:

$$\Delta\theta = -\alpha \cdot \nabla f(\theta_t)$$

**Step 5:** The update rule is therefore:

$$\boxed{\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \alpha \nabla f(\theta_{t})}$$

**Convergence Conditions:**

- **Convexity:** $f$ must be convex (or strongly convex) to guarantee convergence to the global minimum.
- **Smoothness:** Gradient must be $L$-Lipschitz continuous: $\Vert \nabla f(\theta) - \nabla f(\theta') \Vert \leq L \Vert \theta - \theta' \Vert$.
- **Step size:** $\alpha \leq 1/L$ for stable convergence.
- **Stopping criterion:** $\Vert \nabla f(\theta_t) \Vert < \epsilon$ or $\vert f(\theta_{t+1}) - f(\theta_t) \vert < \epsilon$.

For strongly convex $f$, GD achieves **linear convergence** with rate $c = 1 - \alpha \mu$, where $\mu$ is the strong-convexity constant. **(7 marks)**

---

### Part (b) — Gradient Descent for MVO

**Step 1 — Formulation:** The Markowitz problem is:

$$\min_w \quad \frac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w \quad \text{s.t.} \quad w^T \mu = \mu^*, \quad w^T \mathbf{1} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda, \gamma) = \frac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w - \lambda (w^T \mu - \mu^*) - \gamma (w^T \mathbf{1} - 1)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gradient w.r.t. $w$:**

$$\nabla_w \mathcal{L} = \Sigma w - \lambda \mu - \gamma \mathbf{1}$$

**Step 4 — GD Update Rule:** Setting $\nabla_w \mathcal{L}$ as the gradient for minimization:

$$\boxed{w^{(t+1)} = w^{(t)} - \alpha \left( \Sigma w^{(t)} - \lambda \mu - \gamma \mathbf{1} \right)}$$

**Step 5 — Iterative Procedure:**

1. Initialize $w^{(0)}$ and Lagrange multipliers $\lambda, \gamma$.
2. Update weights using the GD rule.
3. Update $\lambda, \gamma$ via gradient ascent (since they are multipliers).
4. Project $w$ back onto the feasible set if constraints are violated.
5. Stop when $\Vert w^{(t+1)} - w^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon$.

**Step 6 — Convergence:** For positive-definite $\Sigma$ (which is guaranteed for non-degenerate assets), the GD sequence converges to:

$$w^* = \lambda \Sigma^{-1} \mu + \gamma \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}$$

which matches the analytical Markowitz solution derived in Section 3.2. **(7 marks)**

---

### 📌 Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the step-by-step working of a Genetic Algorithm. Illustrate with a real-valued portfolio optimization example. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compare Genetic Algorithms with Gradient Descent for portfolio optimization. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other? (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

### Part (a) — GA Workflow with Portfolio Example

**Step 1 — Encoding:** Each chromosome encodes a portfolio as a real-valued weight vector $w = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$, subject to $\sum w_i = 1$ and $w_i \geq 0$. For instance, in a 4-asset problem: $w = (0.25, 0.30, 0.20, 0.25)$.

**Step 2 — Population Initialization:** Generate $N$ random weight vectors satisfying the budget constraint. Example: $N = 5$ chromosomes: $(0.30, 0.20, 0.25, 0.25)$, $(0.15, 0.40, 0.30, 0.15)$, $(0.50, 0.10, 0.20, 0.20)$, $(0.20, 0.30, 0.25, 0.25)$, $(0.40, 0.20, 0.10, 0.30)$.

**Step 3 — Fitness Evaluation:** Use Sharpe Ratio:

$$F(w) = \frac{w^T \mu - r_f}{\sqrt{w^T \Sigma w}}$$

**Step 4 — Selection (Tournament):** For each reproduction event, randomly pick 3 chromosomes; the one with the highest Sharpe Ratio becomes a parent.

**Step 5 — Crossover (Arithmetic):** With probability $p_c = 0.85$, combine two parents:

$$w_{child} = 0.5 \cdot w_{parent1} + 0.5 \cdot w_{parent2}$$

If parent1 $= (0.50, 0.10, 0.20, 0.20)$ and parent2 $= (0.40, 0.20, 0.10, 0.30)$, then child $= (0.45, 0.15, 0.15, 0.25)$.

**Step 6 — Mutation:** With probability $p_m = 0.05$, add Gaussian noise:

$$w_i \leftarrow w_i + \mathcal{N}(0, 0.02^2)$$

Then **renormalize** $w \leftarrow w / \sum w_j$ to enforce the budget constraint.

**Step 7 — Replacement & Elitism:** Replace the worst $N - e$ individuals; carry over the best $e = 1$ unchanged.

**Step 8 — Iterate:** Repeat Steps 3–7 for $G = 100$ generations. Track the best Sharpe Ratio at each generation. Return the best-ever chromosome as the optimal portfolio $w^*$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis

| Criterion | Gradient Descent | Genetic Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Convexity Required** | Yes (MVO is convex → ideal) | No (handles non-convex landscapes) |
| **Discrete Constraints** | Cannot enforce natively | Handles cardinality, lot-size, turnover naturally |
| **Speed per Iteration** | Fast (1 gradient evaluation) | Slow (full population evaluation) |
| **Global Optimality** | Local only | Approximates global |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low (linear algebra) | Moderate (crossover, mutation logic) |
| **Constraint Handling** | Needs projection or KKT | Penalty functions or repair operators |
| **Scalability to Many Assets** | Excellent (matrix inversion scales as $O(n^3)$) | Good (population-based, parallelizable) |
| **Risk of Premature Convergence** | No | Yes (mitigated by mutation and elitism) |

**When to Prefer GD:**

- Problem is smooth and convex (e.g., standard MVO without discrete constraints).
- Very high-dimensional problems where gradient information is cheap.
- Real-time applications needing rapid convergence.

**When to Prefer GA:**

- Problem includes **cardinality constraints** ("select exactly 10 of 500 assets").
- Objective involves **transaction costs** or **discrete lot sizes**.
- **Multi-objective** optimization (Pareto frontiers across return-risk-liquidity).
- **Non-convex** objectives with multiple local minima.
- The function is **non-differentiable** (e.g., with discontinuous transaction cost functions).

**Hybrid Approach (Industry Practice):** Many quantitative firms use **GA for discrete pre-screening** (e.g., choosing the asset universe) followed by **GD for continuous weight refinement** — a powerful combination. **(7 marks)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the GD update rule: writing $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \alpha \nabla f$ instead of $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla f$. This single sign error can cost **2–3 marks** instantly. The rule says move *opposite* to the gradient.
> 
> 2. **Confusing the Lagrangian sign convention**: In MVO, the Lagrangian is $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}w^T \Sigma w - \lambda(w^T \mu - \mu^*) - \gamma(w^T \mathbf{1} - 1)$. Some students flip the signs on $\lambda$ and $\gamma$, which propagates errors throughout.
> 
> 3. **Not normalizing weights after crossover/mutation**: In GA, offspring chromosomes can violate $\sum w_i = 1$. **Always renormalize** before computing fitness, or you will compute meaningless Sharpe Ratios.
> 
> 4. **Stating "GA finds the global optimum"** — GA only *approximates* the global optimum. Examiners will deduct marks for overclaiming. Use the phrase **"approximates the global optimum in probability."**
> 
> 5. **Mixing up the variants**: Writing "SGD = Stochastic Gradient Descent" as "Stochastic Gradient Descent Algorithm" is fine, but confusing it with "Stochastic Gradient BOOTSTRAP" or similar will lose marks. Use precise terminology.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the convergence condition**: Always state that GD converges when $\Vert \nabla f(\theta^*) \Vert = 0$. Examiners reward this completeness.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:

- ✅ **Gradient Descent Update Rule**: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla f(\theta_t)$. Memorize verbatim.

- ✅ **Markowitz Objective**: $\min_w \tfrac{1}{2} w^T \Sigma w$ subject to return and budget constraints.

- ✅ **Closed-Form MVO Weights**: $w^* = \lambda \Sigma^{-1} \mu + \gamma \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}$. Know the constants $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$.

- ✅ **Sharpe Ratio**: $SR = \dfrac{w^T \mu - r_f}{\sqrt{w^T \Sigma w}}$. Higher is better; this is the **fitness function** of choice in GA.

- ✅ **Three GA Operators**: **Selection → Crossover → Mutation → Replacement (with Elitism)**. Know at least one method for each.

- ✅ **Arithmetic Crossover Formula**: $w_{child} = \beta w_{p1} + (1-\beta) w_{p2}$, with $\beta \in [0,1]$.

- ✅ **Gaussian Mutation Formula**: $w_i \leftarrow w_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_m^2)$. Always renormalize after mutation.

- ✅ **GD Convergence Rate**: $\mathcal{O}(1/t)$ for convex smooth; **linear** for strongly convex.

- ✅ **Learning Rate Range**: Typical $\alpha \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$. Optimal $\alpha \approx 1/L$.

- ✅ **GA Hyperparameters**: $N_{pop} \in [50, 200]$, $p_c \in [0.6, 0.95]$, $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$.

- ✅ **When to Use Which**: GD for *convex, smooth* MVO. GA for *non-convex, discrete, cardinality-constrained* problems.

- ✅ **Hybrid Strategy**: GA for asset selection → GD for weight optimization. This is industry-standard.

- ✅ **Key Distinction**: GD uses **gradient information**; GA uses **fitness evaluation only**.

- ✅ **Convexity Assumption**: MVO is a **Quadratic Program (QP)** — globally solvable by convex optimization. Adding real-world frictions (transaction costs, cardinality) makes it **non-convex** — where GA shines.

- ✅ **Year of Origin**: Markowitz MVO — **1952**; GA — John Holland, **1975**.

- ✅ **Sign Convention in Lagrangian**: Constraints enter with a **minus sign**; objective enters **as-is**.

---

## 🎯 Final KTU Examiner's Verdict

> [!TIP]
> **Mastery Strategy**: For a 14-mark question, **always derive, then interpret**. Don't just write the formula — explain *why* the negative sign appears, *why* $\Sigma$ must be positive-definite, and *why* elitism prevents GA from losing its best solution. Examiners at KTU reward **conceptual understanding** over rote memorization.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Optimization Algorithms: Gradient Descent & Genetic Algorithms for Portfolio Optimization.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backtesting Trading Strategies - Python Libraries for Backtesting (e.g., Backtrader, QuantConnect)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT742 - FINANCIAL DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Backtesting Trading Strategies - Python Libraries for Backtesting (e.g., Backtrader, QuantConnect)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Backtesting** is the systematic, retrospective evaluation of a trading strategy's performance by applying its pre-defined entry and exit rules to historical market data. It is formally defined as a quantitative validation framework that simulates the execution of buy/sell signals on a reconstructed historical price series to estimate the strategy's risk-adjusted return characteristics, drawdown profile, and statistical robustness **before** committing real capital.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECDT742 syllabus**, backtesting is positioned as the *empirical bridge* between quantitative strategy design and live deployment. It encompasses three pillars:

1. **Data Ingestion** — feeding OHLCV (Open, High, Low, Close, Volume) historical data.
2. **Strategy Logic Simulation** — replicating broker mechanics, slippage, commissions, and order types.
3. **Performance Attribution** — computing risk-adjusted metrics such as the **Sharpe Ratio (S)**, **Sortino Ratio**, **Maximum Drawdown (MDD)**, and **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition**: Backtesting is **NOT prediction**. It is a counterfactual experiment that answers: *"Would this rule-based logic have made money in the past, under realistic execution assumptions?"*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pilot Simulator"

Imagine a commercial airline pilot. Before flying a real Boeing 737 with 200 passengers, the pilot spends hundreds of hours in a **flight simulator** that recreates turbulence, engine failure, and weather conditions from the past 30 years. The simulator doesn't *guarantee* a safe future flight — but it dramatically increases the probability that the pilot's decision-making will hold up under stress.

Backtesting is the **financial equivalent of that simulator**:

| Real-World Pilot | Backtesting Analogue |
|---|---|
| Cockpit instruments | OHLCV market data |
| Pre-flight checklist | Strategy entry/exit rules |
| Flight simulator | Backtrader / QuantConnect / Zipline |
| Past turbulence logs | Historical crisis periods (2008, 2020) |
| Pilot's reaction | Strategy's signal generation |

## 1.3 Why Dedicated Python Libraries?

Writing a backtester from scratch in NumPy requires manually handling:
- Vectorized vs. event-driven execution
- Position sizing & portfolio accounting
- Survivorship-bias-free data
- Corporate action adjustments (splits, dividends)

Libraries like **Backtrader** and **QuantConnect (LEAN)** encapsulate these engineering concerns so the financial data scientist can focus on the *alpha logic*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Benchmark (Industry Default)**: A robust long-only US equity strategy is expected to deliver a **Sharpe Ratio ≥ 1.0** and **Maximum Drawdown ≤ 20%** to be considered institutionally viable.

## 1.4 The Two Dominant Paradigms

- **Event-Driven (Backtrader, LEAN)**: Iterates bar-by-bar, mimicking a live broker. Highest fidelity. Slower.
- **Vectorized (Pandas, NumPy)**: Computes signals on entire arrays simultaneously. Fastest. Risk: look-ahead bias.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Backtesting Pipeline — Six Logical Phases

The standard pipeline is decomposed into a deterministic sequence of steps:

1. **Hypothesis Formulation**: Define the *edge*. Example: *"Mean-reversion in NIFTY 50 weekly returns."*
2. **Data Acquisition**: Pull clean, adjusted OHLCV data. Apply survivorship-bias correction.
3. **Signal Generation**: Translate hypothesis into deterministic code (e.g., `if RSI(14) < 30 → BUY`).
4. **Execution Simulation**: Apply the next bar's `Open` price (avoid look-ahead). Deduct commissions (e.g., 0.05% per leg) and slippage (e.g., 0.01%).
5. **Portfolio Accounting**: Track cash, position, equity curve $E_t$, and margin.
6. **Performance Attribution**: Compute Sharpe, Sortino, Max Drawdown, CAGR, Win Rate, Profit Factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule**: Signals computed on the **close of bar $t$** must execute at the **open of bar $t+1$**. Violating this is the #1 source of **look-ahead bias** in student submissions.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Periodic Return** | $R_t = \dfrac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}$ | $P_t$ = price at bar $t$ | Decimal |
| 2 | **Cumulative Return** | $R_{cum} = \prod_{t=1}^{N}(1 + R_t) - 1$ | $N$ = total bars | Decimal |
| 3 | **CAGR** | $\text{CAGR} = (1 + R_{cum})^{1/Y} - 1$ | $Y$ = years held | Decimal/Year |
| 4 | **Arithmetic Mean Return** | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N} R_t$ | — | Decimal |
| 5 | **Standard Deviation (Volatility)** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{t=1}^{N}(R_t - \bar{R})^2}$ | — | Decimal |
| 6 | **Sharpe Ratio** | $S = \dfrac{\bar{R} - R_f}{\sigma}$ | $R_f$ = risk-free rate | Unitless |
| 7 | **Annualized Sharpe** | $S_{ann} = S \cdot \sqrt{252}$ | 252 trading days | Unitless |
| 8 | **Downside Deviation** | $\sigma_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{t: R_t < 0} R_t^2}$ | Negative returns only | Decimal |
| 9 | **Sortino Ratio** | $S_o = \dfrac{\bar{R} - R_f}{\sigma_d}$ | — | Unitless |
| 10 | **Maximum Drawdown** | $\text{MDD} = \max_{t}\left(\dfrac{E_t - \max_{s \le t} E_s}{\max_{s \le t} E_s}\right)$ | $E_t$ = equity at time $t$ | Negative Decimal |
| 11 | **Calmar Ratio** | $C = \dfrac{\text{CAGR}}{\vert \text{MDD} \vert}$ | — | Unitless |
| 12 | **Win Rate** | $W = \dfrac{N_{wins}}{N_{trades}}$ | $N_{wins}$ = profitable trades | Decimal |
| 13 | **Profit Factor** | $PF = \dfrac{\sum \text{Gross Profits}}{\sum \text{Gross Losses}}$ | — | Unitless |
| 14 | **Expectancy** | $E = (W \cdot \bar{G}) - ((1-W) \cdot \bar{L})$ | $\bar{G}$=avg win, $\bar{L}$=avg loss | Currency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas above are **mathematically verifiable** and align with the canonical CFA Institute / *Quantitative Trading* (Ernest Chan, 2009) definitions.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

These metrics are not academic curiosities — they are the **gatekeepers of capital allocation** at firms like:
- **Renaissance Technologies** (Medallion Fund): Relies on heavily backtested statistical arbitrage with Sharpe > 3.
- **Two Sigma / Citadel / DE Shaw**: Use event-driven backtesting engines identical in spirit to LEAN/Backtrader.
- **Hedge Fund Risk Committees**: Reject any strategy with MDD > 15% or Sharpe < 0.8.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Annualized Sharpe Ratio from Daily Returns

We start with the raw periodic return series $R_t$ for $t = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ trading days.

**Step 1** — Compute the sample mean daily return.

$$\bar{R} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} R_t$$

*Logic*: This is the central tendency of the strategy's daily P&L. It represents the expected gain per day.

**Step 2** — Compute the sample standard deviation (daily volatility).

$$\sigma_{daily} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{t=1}^{N} (R_t - \bar{R})^2}$$

*Logic*: Bessel's correction ($N-1$) gives an unbiased estimator. The squared deviations penalize both upside and downside variance equally.

**Step 3** — Subtract the daily risk-free rate.

$$R_t^{excess} = R_t - R_f^{daily}$$

*Logic*: The Sharpe Ratio measures return *per unit of risk taken beyond the risk-free alternative* (e.g., a T-Bill).

**Step 4** — Form the daily Sharpe Ratio.

$$S_{daily} = \frac{\bar{R} - R_f^{daily}}{\sigma_{daily}}$$

**Step 5** — Annualize via the **square-root-of-time scaling rule**.

$$S_{ann} = S_{daily} \cdot \sqrt{252}$$

*Logic*: This rule holds **iff** returns are *i.i.d.* (independent and identically distributed). For autocorrelated series (e.g., momentum strategies), it overstates the true Sharpe. Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}\left(\sum_{t=1}^{252} R_t\right) &= \sum_{t=1}^{252} \text{Var}(R_t) + 2\sum_{i<j} \text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) \\
&= 252 \sigma_{daily}^2 \quad \text{(if independent)} \\
\sigma_{annual} &= \sqrt{252} \cdot \sigma_{daily} \\
\Rightarrow S_{ann} &= \frac{\sqrt{252} \cdot \bar{R}_{daily}}{\sqrt{252} \cdot \sigma_{daily}} = S_{daily} \cdot \sqrt{252}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

A student backtests a mean-reversion strategy on RELIANCE (NSE) for 252 trading days. The strategy yields:

- Mean daily return: $\bar{R} = 0.0008$ (i.e., 0.08%/day)
- Daily volatility: $\sigma_{daily} = 0.012$ (i.e., 1.2%/day)
- Daily risk-free rate: $R_f^{daily} = 0.0002$ (≈ 5% annualized)

**Compute the annualized Sharpe Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{daily} &= \frac{0.0008 - 0.0002}{0.012} \\
&= \frac{0.0006}{0.012} \\
&= 0.05
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{ann} &= 0.05 \cdot \sqrt{252} \\
&= 0.05 \cdot 15.8745 \\
&= 0.7937
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: A Sharpe of **0.79** is *sub-institutional*. The student should be cautioned that this is below the typical hedge fund benchmark of **1.0**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Drawdown Algorithmically

Given equity curve $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_T$:

**Step 1** — Compute the running peak (high-water mark).

$$P_t = \max_{s \le t} E_s$$

**Step 2** — Compute the drawdown series.

$$D_t = \frac{E_t - P_t}{P_t}$$

**Step 3** — MDD is the worst drawdown (most negative value).

$$\text{MDD} = \min_{t \in [1, T]} D_t \quad \text{(always} \le 0\text{)}$$

**Numerical Illustration:**

| Day | Equity $E_t$ | Peak $P_t$ | Drawdown $D_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100,000 | 100,000 | 0.00% |
| 2 | 105,000 | 105,000 | 0.00% |
| 3 | 102,000 | 105,000 | -2.86% |
| 4 | 98,000 | 105,000 | -6.67% |
| 5 | 99,000 | 105,000 | -5.71% |

$$\text{MDD} = -6.67\% \text{ (on Day 4)}$$

## 3.4 Backtrader Architecture — Detailed Workflow

The Backtrader framework operates on a **Cerebro engine** that orchestrates the following components:

1. **Data Feeds**: CSV, Pandas, Yahoo Finance, Interactive Brokers.
2. **Strategy Class**: Subclass of `bt.Strategy`; defines `next()` method called per bar.
3. **Sizers**: Determine position size (fixed, all-in, percentage).
4. **Brokers**: Simulate cash, margin, commission schemes.
5. **Analyzers**: Compute Sharpe, DrawDown, TradeAnalyzer, SQN (System Quality Number).
6. **Observers**: Plot equity curve, buy/sell markers, indicator overlays.

## 3.5 QuantConnect (LEAN) Architecture

LEAN is a **cloud-native, event-driven** engine written in C# with Python wrappers. Key features:

- **Algorithm Framework**: `Alpha`, `Portfolio`, `Execution`, `Risk` models separated.
- **Universe Selection**: Coarse & fine fundamental filters.
- **Coarse Universes**: Top 1000 by liquidity.
- **Fine Universes**: Fundamentals filter (P/E, market cap, sector).
- **Slippage Models**: `VolumeShareSlippageModel`, `ConstantSlippageModel`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Backtesting Workflow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Strategy Hypothesis] --> B[Acquire Historical OHLCV Data]
    B --> C[Clean Data: Handle Splits, Dividends, Missing Values]
    C --> D[Compute Indicators: SMA, RSI, MACD]
    D --> E{Generate Signal at Bar t}
    E -->|BUY| F[Place Order at Open of Bar t+1]
    E -->|SELL| G[Place Order at Open of Bar t+1]
    E -->|HOLD| H[Carry Forward Position]
    F --> I[Apply Commission and Slippage]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Update Portfolio: Cash and Equity]
    J --> K[Record Equity Curve E sub t]
    K --> L{All Bars Processed?}
    L -->|No| E
    L -->|Yes| M[Compute Performance Metrics]
    M --> N[Sharpe Ratio]
    M --> O[Sortino Ratio]
    M --> P[Max Drawdown]
    M --> Q[CAGR and Calmar Ratio]
    N --> R[Validation: Walk Forward, Out of Sample]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Decision: Deploy Live or Refine]
```

## 4.2 Backtrader Internal Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph engine["Cerebro Engine Core"]
        DF[Data Feeds: CSV, Yahoo, IB]
        STR[Strategy Class with next method]
        SZ[Sizers: Position Sizing Logic]
        BK[Broker: Cash, Commission, Margin]
        AN[Analyzers: Sharpe, DrawDown, SQN]
        OB[Observers: Equity Curve Plotter]
    end
    DF --> STR
    STR --> SZ
    SZ --> BK
    BK --> OB
    STR --> AN
    AN --> RES[Final Performance Report]
    OB --> RES
```

## 4.3 QuantConnect LEAN Engine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph leanchain["LEAN Execution Chain"]
        ALG[Algorithm Python Wrapper]
        UNV[Universe Selection Module]
        ALPHA[Alpha Model: Signal Generation]
        PORT[Portfolio Model: Position Targets]
        EXEC[Execution Model: Order Routing]
        RISK[Risk Model: Exposure Limits]
        DATAFEED[Historical Data and Live Stream]
    end
    DATAFEED --> UNV
    UNV --> ALPHA
    ALPHA --> PORT
    PORT --> EXEC
    EXEC --> RISK
    RISK --> FEEDBACK[Send Orders to Simulated Broker]
    FEEDBACK --> ALG
```

## 4.4 Look-Ahead Bias Prevention Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant BarT as Bar t (Close Time)
    participant Signal as Signal Logic
    participant Order as Pending Order
    participant BarT1 as Bar t+1 (Open Time)
    BarT->>Signal: Compute RSI, MACD using Close sub t
    Signal->>Order: Generate BUY or SELL signal
    Order->>BarT1: Execute at Open sub t+1
    Note over BarT1: This prevents look-ahead bias
    BarT1->>BarT1: Update Portfolio Equity
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Backtesting. List any four performance metrics used to evaluate a backtested strategy. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Backtesting is the process of evaluating a trading strategy by simulating its execution on historical market data to estimate its risk-adjusted performance prior to live deployment.

**Four Performance Metrics (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Sharpe Ratio** — measures excess return per unit of total volatility.
2. **Sortino Ratio** — variant that penalizes only downside volatility.
3. **Maximum Drawdown (MDD)** — the largest peak-to-trough equity decline.
4. **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)** — geometric mean annual return.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Event-Driven and Vectorized Backtesting. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Event-Driven (Backtrader, LEAN) | Vectorized (Pandas/NumPy) |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution Style** | Bar-by-bar iteration simulating live broker | Bulk computation on entire arrays |
| **Realism** | High — handles slippage, partial fills | Lower — risk of look-ahead bias |
| **Speed** | Slower (Python overhead per bar) | Fastest (compiled C under NumPy) |
| **Use Case** | Production-grade strategies, live deployment | Research, parameter sweeps, prototyping |
| **Memory** | Lower (one bar at a time) | Higher (entire dataset in RAM) |

(Any three valid distinctions = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Backtest Analysis `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the architecture of the Backtrader framework. With a neat diagram, describe the role of Cerebro, Strategy, Broker, Sizer, and Analyzer.**

**Model Solution:**

**Cerebro (2 Marks):** The central engine that orchestrates the entire backtest. It manages data feeds, strategy instances, brokers, sizers, analyzers, and observers. `cerebro.run()` triggers the simulation.

**Strategy (2 Marks):** A subclass of `bt.Strategy`. The user overrides `next(self)` — invoked once per bar — to compute indicators and call `self.buy()` / `self.sell()`. Encapsulates alpha logic.

**Broker (1 Mark):** Simulates a real broker: tracks cash, position, margin, applies commission schemes (e.g., `bt.broker.schemes.CommInfoBase`).

**Sizer (1 Mark):** Determines how many shares/contracts to trade. Options include `FixedSize`, `PercentSizer`, `AllInSizer`.

**Analyzer (1 Mark):** Post-run computation modules. Examples: `bt.analyzers.SharpeRatio`, `bt.analyzers.DrawDown`, `bt.analyzers.TradeAnalyzer`.

**Diagram**: Refer to Section 4.2 above.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: A strategy generates daily returns with mean = 0.0012, standard deviation = 0.015, and the daily risk-free rate is 0.0003. Compute the annualized Sharpe Ratio and interpret the result.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Compute daily Sharpe.

$$S_{daily} = \frac{0.0012 - 0.0003}{0.015} = \frac{0.0009}{0.015} = 0.06$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Annualize.

$$S_{ann} = 0.06 \cdot \sqrt{252} = 0.06 \cdot 15.8745 = 0.9525$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Interpretation.

A Sharpe of **0.95** is *borderline institutional*. While above the average hedge fund Sharpe (~0.5), it falls just short of the **1.0 benchmark** considered the gold standard for long/short equity strategies. The student should consider:
- Higher turnover (intraday data, 252×$\sqrt{252}$ scaling) or
- Lower volatility via volatility-targeted position sizing.

**Final Answer (1 Mark):** Sharpe ≈ **0.95 — marginally below institutional threshold**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative — QuantConnect & Advanced Metrics `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Describe the LEAN engine architecture of QuantConnect. Explain the role of Alpha, Portfolio, Execution, and Risk models with a block diagram.**

**Model Solution:**

**LEAN (Lean Engine Algorithmic Trading) (1 Mark):** An open-source, event-driven algorithmic trading engine powering QuantConnect's cloud platform.

**Alpha Model (2 Marks):** Generates *insights* — predicted directions with confidence levels and magnitudes. Example: `EmaCrossAlphaModel` emits UP insights when fast EMA crosses above slow EMA.

**Portfolio Model (2 Marks):** Translates insights into *target portfolio weights* and rebalances. Examples: `MeanVarianceOptimizationPortfolioModel`, `EqualWeightingPortfolioModel`.

**Execution Model (1 Mark):** Converts target weights into *actual market orders*. Handles market impact, slippage models (`VolumeShareSlippageModel`), and order routing.

**Risk Model (1 Mark):** Enforces constraints: max leverage, sector exposure, single-position concentration. Prevents catastrophic drawdowns.

**Diagram**: Refer to Section 4.3.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Given the following equity curve values (in ₹): [100000, 105000, 102000, 98000, 99000, 103000, 108000], compute the Maximum Drawdown and Calmar Ratio assuming CAGR = 12%.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Running Peak (2 Marks):**

| Day | Equity | Running Peak $P_t$ | Drawdown $D_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100,000 | 100,000 | 0.00% |
| 2 | 105,000 | 105,000 | 0.00% |
| 3 | 102,000 | 105,000 | -2.86% |
| 4 | 98,000 | 105,000 | **-6.67%** |
| 5 | 99,000 | 105,000 | -5.71% |
| 6 | 103,000 | 105,000 | -1.90% |
| 7 | 108,000 | 108,000 | 0.00% |

**Step 2 — Identify MDD (2 Marks):**

$$\text{MDD} = -6.67\%$$

**Step 3 — Apply Calmar Formula (2 Marks):**

$$C = \frac{\text{CAGR}}{|\text{MDD}|} = \frac{0.12}{0.0667} = 1.80$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 Mark):**

A Calmar Ratio of **1.80** is considered *excellent* in the Indian mid-cap universe. It indicates that the CAGR is 1.8× larger than the worst drawdown — a healthy risk-reward profile.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Backtesting Questions:**
>
> 1. **Look-Ahead Bias** (–2 marks): Using the *same bar's close* for both signal and execution. Always execute on the *next bar's open*.
> 2. **Forgetting to Annualize** (–1 mark): Reporting a *daily* Sharpe as the *annual* Sharpe. Always multiply by $\sqrt{252}$ (equity) or $\sqrt{252 \cdot 6.5}$ (intraday).
> 3. **Confusing Sortino and Sharpe Denominators** (–1 mark): Sortino uses *downside deviation* ($\sigma_d$), not total $\sigma$.
> 4. **MDD Sign Convention** (–1 mark): MDD is reported as a *negative* number or its absolute value — be consistent and state the convention.
> 5. **Ignoring Transaction Costs** (–2 marks): A strategy with no commission/slippage assumption can inflate returns by 200–400 bps annually — examiners explicitly deduct for unrealistic assumptions.
> 6. **Survivorship Bias in Universe** (–2 marks): Using only currently-listed stocks (e.g., current NIFTY 50 constituents) when backtesting 2010 falsely inflates returns by ~1–2% per year.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Recall Points for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- ✅ **Backtesting = retrospective simulation**, not prediction. The objective is *risk-adjusted performance estimation* and *strategy validation* — not forecasting.
- ✅ **Event-driven libraries (Backtrader, LEAN)** process data bar-by-bar, mimicking live broker mechanics. **Vectorized approaches (Pandas)** process entire arrays at once but are prone to look-ahead bias.
- ✅ **Backtrader's `Cerebro`** is the central engine; the `next()` method in the Strategy class is the *alpha logic injection point*.
- ✅ **QuantConnect's LEAN** separates concerns into Alpha, Portfolio, Execution, and Risk models — a clean production-grade architecture.
- ✅ **Sharpe Ratio** formula: $S = \dfrac{\bar{R} - R_f}{\sigma}$. Annualized: $S_{ann} = S_{daily} \cdot \sqrt{252}$.
- ✅ **Sortino Ratio** uses *downside deviation* $\sigma_d$ in the denominator — it ignores upside volatility.
- ✅ **Maximum Drawdown (MDD)** is the worst peak-to-trough decline. Always reported as a **negative** percentage.
- ✅ **CAGR** is the geometric mean annual return: $\text{CAGR} = (1 + R_{cum})^{1/Y} - 1$.
- ✅ **Calmar Ratio** = CAGR / |MDD| — a drawdown-adjusted return metric.
- ✅ **Profit Factor** = Gross Profits / Gross Losses. A value **> 1.5** is considered robust.
- ✅ **Look-ahead bias prevention rule**: Signal at close of bar $t$ → execute at open of bar $t+1$.
- ✅ **Transaction cost realism**: Always include commissions (0.05%) and slippage (0.01%) in institutional-grade backtests.
- ✅ **Out-of-sample testing (Walk-Forward Analysis)** is mandatory to avoid overfitting. Train on 70%, test on 30% held-out data.
- ✅ **Institutional benchmarks**: Sharpe ≥ 1.0, MDD ≤ 20%, Calmar ≥ 1.0, Profit Factor ≥ 1.5.
- ✅ **Common pitfalls**: Survivorship bias, data-snooping bias, regime change, ignoring corporate actions (splits/dividends).
